chapters.summary: Council staff opens the joint hearing of the Committees on Economic Development and Workforce Development, reminds attendees to silence devices and fill out appearance cards to testify.
chapters.title: Staff opens hearing with housekeeping announcements

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Welcome to today's New York City Council hearing for the Committee on Economic Development, joint with the Committee on Workforce Development.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please silence all cell phone and electronic devices.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: If you wish to testify, please fill out an appearance card with the sergeant at arms.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Moving forward, no one is to approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chair, we are ready to begin.

chapters.summary: Chair Maloney introduces the joint hearing, explains the Green Economy Action Plan was released in February 2024 by EDC and NYC Talent, identified 133,000 existing green jobs, and set a target of 400,000 green jobs by 2040. She notes no permanent EDC leader has been appointed and the new administration has not stated whether it will preserve the plan.
chapters.title: Chair Maloney introduces hearing and Green Economy Action Plan background

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: All right, good afternoon everyone and welcome to the joint hearing of the Committees on Economic Development and Workforce Development.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Today is Friday, June 26th.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I'm Virginia Maloney and I chair the Economic Development Committee.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Joining me is Councilmember Julie Won, Chair of the Committee on Workforce Development.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Today, we are here to ask about the state of the Green Economy Action Plan and if it remains the city's strategy to generate green jobs.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: The plan was drafted together by EDC and NYC Talent and was released in February 2024 under the last administration.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: It identified 133,000 jobs that exist in the city and set a target for creating nearly 400,000 green jobs by 2040.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Since no permanent EDC leader has been appointed since then, we're unclear about what the progress is, if any has been made regarding the plan.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: The new administration was not involved in developing the plan and has not stated whether or not it intends to preserve or advance it.

chapters.summary: Maloney notes Congress stripped federal funding the plan relied on, and that the Center for an Urban Future found only 2,000 green jobs added since 2023, meaning the 400,000 target would not be reached until well into the next century at that rate.
chapters.title: Maloney on federal funding stripped and job creation gap

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And additionally, Congress has stripped away much of the federal funding that the plan was built around.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So given the change in circumstances, the committees want to explore whether or not the Green Economy Action Plan remains viable and whether or not it had

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: has the resources necessary to implement.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Beginning with jobs, the city projected 400,000 green jobs by 2040, and that means we would have to create 267,000 green jobs over the next 14 years.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: However, the Center for an Urban Future found that the city added

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: roughly 2,000 jobs since 2023.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: At that rate, we would not hit the target until well into the next century.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So today we want to understand how the administration reconciles these numbers and whether or not the goal still remains and how it's publicly tracking against progress.

chapters.summary: Maloney highlights the South Brooklyn Marine Terminal as the most visible plan component, with Empire Wind projecting 13,000 jobs by 2035. She notes the federal stop order on Empire Wind in December and asks how EDC is preparing for possible construction halts.
chapters.title: Maloney on offshore wind, SBMT, and Empire Wind uncertainty

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Second, we want to dig into the offshore wind project, which is the most visible component of this plan.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: The city made significant investments in South Brooklyn Marine Terminal to serve as the assembly hub for Empire Wind, projecting the creation of 13,000 jobs across the city by 2035.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Empire Wind was intended to demonstrate the core premise of the Green Economy Action Plan, proving that climate investments can create good-paying local jobs.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: But the project faces uncertainty, with the federal government issuing a stop order on Empire Wind in December, and construction has only continued because the court intervened.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So the committees are seeking...

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Clear answer on what the city's oversight is at the South Brooklyn Marine Terminal and how EDC is preparing for the possibility

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: that construction there might be halted again.

chapters.summary: Maloney addresses Congress rolling back clean energy tax credits, then pivots to building decarbonization as more than half of projected green jobs, dependent on Local Law 97 enforcement. She thanks committee staff and hands to Chair Won.
chapters.title: Maloney on federal rollback, building decarbonization, and Local Law 97

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Unfortunately, the federal government's actions undercut much of the intentions of the plan.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Last summer, Congress rolled back the clean energy tax credits that much of this plan relied on.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So when EDC says certain commitments are still on track,

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thrilled to hear that, but want to understand a bit more about where the funding is coming from.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And lastly, building decarbonization or lowering a building's carbon footprint accounts for more than half of the green jobs that are projected in this plan and depends on the successful implementation of Local Law 97.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: By setting carbon emissions for large buildings, Local Law 97 creates the demand for retrofits and the workforce needed to complete them.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Meeting the employment targets outlined in the plan assumes that Local Law 97 is being enforced and implemented on schedule, and we want to discuss the progress of those building retrofits to date, how many jobs are currently in the pipeline,

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: and how we are supporting decarbonization across the city.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Before we hear from the administration, I want to thank the committee staff for their hard work, starting with Senior Counsel Alex Polonov, Senior Policy Analyst William Hongnank, and Financial Analyst Spencer Kuhn.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And with that, I will turn to Chair Wan for her opening remarks.

chapters.summary: Chair Won acknowledges joined council members and outlines the plan's three workforce promises: green collar training centers in every borough, 12,000 New Yorkers trained in green jobs by 2040, and a $600 million investment in green career pathways. She questions whether the $600 million actually funds green training or is relabeled existing spending.
chapters.title: Chair Won on workforce promises and $600M green funding question

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair Maloney.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: I want to acknowledge that we've been joined by Councilmember Amanda Farías, as well as Councilmember Shanel-Thomas-Henry.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: My name is Julie Won, and I have the privilege of chairing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Committee on Workforce Development.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Chair Maloney has laid out the economic development side of this plan.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: I want to talk about the parts that matter the most to the work themselves, which is whether the city is actually building a path for New Yorkers into these jobs.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: A plan that promises 400,000 green jobs by 2040 means very little of the people who need these jobs cannot get trained for them.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: The plan made three major workforce promises.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: First, a green collar training center in every single borough.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Second, for more than 12,000 New Yorkers trained in screen jobs through apprenticeships and pre-apprenticeship programs by 2040,

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: And third, what the plan calls a $600 million investment in green career pathways for young people.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: I want to know how many of these promises have been kept and the numbers behind them broken down by fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: I also want to be precise about the $600 million since it doesn't come from the Green Economy Action Plan.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: It's our understanding that it comes from the city's 2023 Pathways to an Inclusive Economy Plan, and it is spread across public schools, CUNY, DYCD, to serve about 250,000 young people across the entire economy, not just green jobs.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So how much of that $600 million actually funds the green training?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: And is there any green-specific money in this plan at all?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Or are we just relabeling spending that was already happening for PR purposes?

chapters.summary: Won raises concerns about WIOA reauthorization folding workforce programs into block grants with $1.2 billion in cuts, and questions how the Mamdani administration's new $4.5 million green workforce pilot relates to the existing plan, asking whether the plan is being quietly shelved.
chapters.title: Won on federal WIOA risks and Mamdani $4.5M pilot overlap

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Federal funding is another problem.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: The Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act has been reauthorized.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Rise and the federal budget for next year would fold workforce programs into a block grant and cut more than 1.2 billion from existing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: programs.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: A lot of the city's trainings run on those federal dollars, so we want to know how much of the green training is in this plan and depend on federal dollars that now it's at risk and what is the administration's will for prioritizing to keep this money if it disappears.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Separately, the Mamdani administration just launched its own $4.5 million green workforce program as a pilot with the DOE with the DOE fund.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: We don't object to the pilot, but we want to know how the pilot is going to be impacting the Green Economy Action Plan, because it sends a signal when a new administration launches its own green jobs program instead of funding the one already on the books.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: We want to know whether the plan is being quietly shelved, and if not, we want to know who's making sure that these programs aren't tripping over each other, and how do they work in tandem with each other, and how are the responsibilities or portfolios divided up.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: What we will be looking for today is evidence from EDC and New York City talent that the New Yorkers this plan was written for are actually getting trained and hired into good-paying jobs, stable green jobs.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Before we turn over to the administration, I'd like to thank and acknowledge the Workforce Development Committee staff, Senior Counsel Alex Polonov, Senior Policy Analyst William Hognick, and Financial Analyst Spencer Kuhn.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: With that, I'll turn over to the Committee Council to administer the oath, and I also want to acknowledge that we've been joined by Councilmember Caban and Councilmember Hanif.

chapters.summary: Committee counsel Alex Polanoff administers the oath to administration witnesses Sam Jung (EDC) and Douglas Perry (NYC Talent), who affirm to tell the truth.
chapters.title: Staff administers oath to administration witnesses

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Juan.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Alex Polanoff, Committee Counsel.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Will all members of the administration testifying today please raise your hands?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you swear or affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth in your testimony today, and to respond honestly to Council Member questions?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Yes, I do.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin when ready.

chapters.summary: Sam Jung, SVP of the Green Economy Team at EDC, introduces himself and Michelle Lamberti. He frames the green economy as a climate imperative and pathway to economic justice under Mayor Mamdani, citing extreme weather impacts on the city. He describes the GEEP as a roadmap projecting 400,000 green jobs by 2040 across construction, engineering, clean energy, and other sectors.
chapters.title: Sam Jung (EDC) on climate context and GEEP overview with 400K projection

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Maloney and Chair Wan, and members of the Economic Development and Workforce Development Committees.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: My name is Sam Jung, and I serve as the Senior Vice President of the Green Economy Team at the New York City Economic Development Corporation.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I'm here today with Michelle Lamberti, Vice President of Government and Community Relations.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I'm excited to share the progress we've made on the Green Economy Action Plan, or GEEP for short, which was released in spring of 2024 in partnership with New York City Talent.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: At EDC, we think in terms of decades and generational change.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Under Mayor Mumdani and Deputy Mayor Su's leadership, EDC is approaching the green economy not only as a climate imperative, but as a pathway to economic justice, delivering good-paying jobs and expanding opportunity for working people.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: New York City is surrounded by 520 miles of water and shoreline, with the risk of rising seawater on our doorstep.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We have seen a hurricane leave our city dark and underwater.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Heavy rain and flooding have disrupted our transportation and infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Urban wildfires have stained our skyline red and filled our air with ashes.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And extreme weather has driven up power bills and insurance costs for working people.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We cannot treat climate change as something we can put off until tomorrow.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: It requires...

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: action today.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The Green Economy Action Plan provides a roadmap to tackle these challenges and invest in the sectors and jobs that will help the city combat climate change while providing a pathway to stronger communities and shared prosperity.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The opportunity ahead is significant.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The Jeep projected that the green economy could transition and create approximately 400,000 jobs by 2040.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Jobs across construction, engineering, clean energy, building retrofits, advanced manufacturing, design, operations, maintenance, and emerging industries.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Sorry, could you put the mic closer to your mouth?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Jung clarifies that 70% of the 400,000 projected jobs are transitioning jobs that won't be trackable for years, and that the projection depends on local, state, and federal climate policies, public and private investments, and consumer preferences. He acknowledges federal rollbacks are having an impact but says EDC takes a forward-looking approach.
chapters.title: Jung on 70% transitioning jobs and federal headwinds

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I want to be clear, realizing the 2040 job projections will take decades.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: 70% of the 400,000 jobs projected are what we call transitioning jobs, which we won't be able to track until years down the road.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: These jobs do not appear automatically or overnight.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: They require planning.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The Jeep is a projection of the long-term transformation of our economy, the result of sustained public investment from every level of government, city, state, and federal, as well as private investment, industrial development, and workforce growth.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Yet, we're continuing to face real challenges from federal headwinds.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Like the rest of the nation, job growth is slowing and aggressive federal rollbacks of key green economy policies are having an impact.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Despite this, we take a forward-looking approach to growth, development, and the green economy.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: In the two years since its release, EDC has been focused on meeting key commitments from the Jeep, advancing major green infrastructure investments, and activating workforce partners across the city to prepare for the future.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: prepare New Yorkers for the economy of the future.

chapters.summary: Jung highlights EDC's $7 million investment in CUNY green workforce training, $3 million Greenlight Innovation Fund award with Councilmember Feliz for the Bronx Green Job Center at Fordham, and the Solar One environmental education center ribbon-cutting at Stuyvesant Cove Park.
chapters.title: Jung on workforce training investments: CUNY, Bronx Green Job Center, Solar One

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Today, I'll use my time to share a handful of updates on this work.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: New Yorkers have always used innovation and ingenuity to respond to whatever challenges come our way, and we have flourished because we have invested in our people.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: A just and equitable transition to the green economy means creating accessible career pathways for working people.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: At EDC, we are supercharging our efforts to educate, train, and position New Yorkers of all backgrounds to benefit from the green economy.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Earlier this year, we announced a $7 million investment into CUNY's green workforce training infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: This $7 million investment will ensure our students graduate into a job market with the skills to change the world for the better.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Get a good paying job, and cement New York City as a leader in climate action.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: In July 2025, we awarded $3 million from our Greenlight Innovation Fund, alongside a $1.1 million grant from Councilmember Oswald Feliz, to Fordham University to create the Bronx Green Job Center,

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: a first-of-its-kind workforce and entrepreneurship hub that will build an equitable green jobs pipeline in the heart of the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And last September, EDC joined Solar One to cut the ribbon on new state-of-the-art environmental education center in Stuyvesant Cove Park.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: A decade in the making, this education center will be a model for future urban coastal resiliency, while also creating an immersive, hands-on environmental learning experience for New Yorkers of all ages.

chapters.summary: Jung describes the Maritime Career and Aviation Awareness Fair connecting 500 high school students with industry experts, the Waterfront Pathways program for MWBEs, and B-Source NYC helping local companies secure $13 million in offshore wind contracts, now expanding to building electrification.
chapters.title: Jung on career pathways, Maritime Fair, Waterfront Pathways, B-Source NYC

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We are also creating career paths for young people through our Maritime Career and Aviation Awareness Fair.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We hosted the seventh annual fair.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: this year and connected more than 500 high school students with industry experts on potential future careers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We were making significant investments to prepare local workers and New Yorkers from all backgrounds and businesses for the transition to the green economy.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Our Waterfront Pathways program is helping address disparities in public procurement by expanding opportunities for MWBEs and waterfront industries.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And through B-Source NYC, we are supporting local small and medium-sized industrial businesses to capture contract opportunities in the offshore wind supply chain.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The first year of the program saw eight local companies secure over $13 million in offshore wind contracts.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The second year of this program is focused on supporting local general, mechanical, electrical, and plumbing contractors to expand within the growing building electrification sector.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And our economic mobility networks in Sunset Park and Hunts Point, as well as the East Brooklyn Workforce Development Fund and the Staten Island North Shore Workforce Fund, are helping more New Yorkers and waterfront communities get on a pathway to a good paying career.

chapters.summary: Jung covers coastal resiliency projects including Lower Manhattan and Eastside Coastal Resilience, then describes the Brooklyn Army Terminal as a world-class climate innovation hub. Batworks will house the largest green workforce training facility in NYC at 30,000 square feet, with a temporary space launched in May and permanent hub completion in 2028.
chapters.title: Jung on coastal resiliency and Brooklyn Army Terminal/Batworks hub

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Over the past two years, we have also advanced coastal resiliency projects to protect waterfront communities, residents, workers, and businesses, including the Lower Manhattan Coastal Resilience Project, the Eastside Coastal Resiliency Project, among countless others throughout the city.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: At EDC, we see the Green Economy Action Plan as both a climate strategy and an economic blueprint for equitable growth.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: It is a framework for ensuring that the technologies, companies, and workers driving the clean energy transition can thrive in the decades to come.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We want to ensure that the next great climate change

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: technologies are not only simply being invented in New York City, but that they are built, tested, deployed, and scaled here.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And that work is being shaped at places like the Brooklyn Army Terminal, where we are building a world-class climate innovation hub.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Climate challenges are not solved by one sector alone.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: They require collaboration between government, businesses, workers, and communities.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: At Brooklyn Army Terminal, we are embarking on a new historic chapter, supporting the hundreds of businesses, industrial manufacturers, and thousands of employees who currently call it home,

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: and anchoring it with a growing ecosystem for new climate technologists and entrepreneurs working on solutions to combat climate change.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Batworks will also be home to the largest green workforce training facility in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Connecting New Yorkers, including residents in the Sunset Park community, to real economic opportunity while supporting green industrial growth.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: This former military campus is now on the front lines of the next fight of our lives, leading the line on climate innovation.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Earlier this May, we launched the Batworks Temporary Space, creating an on-the-ground presence today.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We are bringing the Sunset Park community and industry together to connect, learn, and help shape Batworks and shape Batworks activities ahead of the permanent hub's completion in 2028,

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: and accelerating the city's $100 million investment to ensure its benefits and jobs reach more New Yorkers and more communities.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Small businesses and startups are the future of the green economy.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And at Batworks, we are supporting their growth through programs like Pilots at Bat and our Offshore Wind Innovation Hub programming.

chapters.summary: Jung describes the Harbor Climate Collaborative (HCC) with Trust for Governors Island and Brooklyn Navy Yard, supporting emerging businesses and training students. He notes transportation accounts for 25% of city emissions and EDC is building sustainable alternatives.
chapters.title: Jung on Harbor Climate Collaborative and sustainable transportation

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We are also advancing Jeep through the harbor.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: or Climate Collaborative, or HCC, a joint initiative with the Trust for Governors Island and Brooklyn Navy Yard Development Corporation.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: At Sunset Park at the Brooklyn Navy Yard and Governors Island, the HCC is supporting emerging businesses and companies, helping existing businesses scale, preparing New Yorkers for good paying jobs, and training our students for the future jobs in the green economy.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The Green Economy Action Plan goes far beyond education and job training.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: It also lays out how we can deliver more sustainable infrastructure for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Today, transportation makes up about 25% of New York City's emissions.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: At EDC, we are building public goods for the public good, working to deliver more sustainable alternatives in transportation.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: That not only make our city more livable, but deliver good paying jobs for working people.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: New York City has always been a maritime city, and we are charting a new course for our economic future, using our historic waterways to create sustainable infrastructure that moves goods off the streets and onto our waterways, reducing pollution and making our city cleaner, healthier, and more sustainable.

chapters.summary: Jung discusses the Brooklyn Marine Terminal as a hub for the Blue Highways waterborne freight network, the Hunts Point Marine Terminal vision plan, electric air taxi demonstration flights at the downtown Skyport, and the first community-driven freight-focused EV charging depot at Hunts Point.
chapters.title: Jung on Blue Highways, EV charging, and electric air taxi

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We are advancing transformative projects at the Brooklyn Marine Terminal, which will become a critical hub for New York's Blue Highways waterborne freight network.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And last year, we released the vision plan for Hunts Point Marine Terminal, another critical link in our Blue Highways network.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: In April this year, we took transportation and sustainability to new heights.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: With the first ever point-to-point electric vertical takeoff and landing air taxi demonstration flights in New York City's history.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: At the downtown Skyport in Manhattan, we are building a low-carbon, multimodal working waterfront where freight will move goods off our railroads.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: roads and onto our waterways, creating green jobs while leading the world on the adoption of safe, electrified flight in an urban environment.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The build out of this infrastructure is moving ahead on time and we expect construction to begin this year.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And we are keeping our city moving by expanding our EV charging infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Last year, we unveiled New York City's first community

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: community-driven freight-focused EV charging depot at Hunts Point Distribution Center, advancing the city's transition to cleaner and more efficient ground transportation of goods.

chapters.summary: Jung addresses construction emissions (70% of city emissions), the Mass Timber Studio initiative for low-carbon construction as part of Mayor Mamdani's housing plan, and circular design and construction guidelines encouraging material reuse and lifecycle planning.
chapters.title: Jung on building decarbonization, Mass Timber, and circular design

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Construction has historically been one of the largest contributors to emissions and waste, with 70% of emissions in our city coming from our buildings.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: But the buildings of tomorrow cannot be built with the assumptions of yesterday.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: At EDC, we are shifting how we think about construction, moving from extractive systems towards regenerative ones.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: A great example of this work is our Mass Timber Studio initiative, which is advancing the use of low-carbon construction.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: This year, we are launching the third cohort of our Mass Timber Studio as part of Mayor Mumdani's block-to-block housing plan to deliver more affordable housing to New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: This work will support a pipeline of new housing projects that will be delivered more efficiently and sustainably.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The studio will train and support project development teams in the technical components of modular delivery and provide regulatory support in partnership with DOB and the fire department.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The promise of mass timber is connected to a promise of economic, environmental, and housing justice.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Creating homes that teachers, workers, young families, and long-time residents can actually afford, and expanding opportunities for architects.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: engineers, contractors, and skilled trades workers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: But we know that this innovation must be paired with standards.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: That is why EDC has supported the development of circular design and construction guidelines, a framework that encourages reuse, material recovery, and designing buildings with their entire life cycle in mind.

chapters.summary: Jung describes Spark Kips Bay as a life sciences and health tech hub with 95% material diversion from landfills, salvaging 76 tons of waste. He then covers Willets Point's transformation from a floodplain into a new neighborhood with 2,500 affordable homes, green roofs, new infrastructure, and the fully electric Etihad Stadium for NYCFC and the 2028 Olympics.
chapters.title: Jung on Spark Kips Bay deconstruction and Willets Point development

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We see this future taking shape in projects like Spark Kips Bay, where we are transforming an entire New York City block into a state-of-the-art and first-of-its-kind education, job, innovation hub focused on life sciences and health tech.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Last year, we began deconstruction on the site, and we are on track to divert an unprecedented 95% of project materials from landfills.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: So far, we have salvaged and diverted 76 tons of waste, repurposing hundreds of light fixtures, masonry block, wood paneling, bluestone pavers, historic relief sculptures, and iron gates.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And in Queens at Willits Point, we are pairing affordability and economic justice with sustainability, so housing and new businesses can take root.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Located within a floodplain, we have said goodbye to the Valley of Ashes, to the pollution, decay, and neglect that has defined this area for generations, and in its place, built a brand new neighborhood

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: with 2,500 new affordable houses and residential buildings seeking to achieve high standards of environmental sustainability, including green roofs.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We have built new infrastructure like sewers and stormwater drains, and we have raised new streets and buildings out of the floodplain to protect residents from future storms.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The first residents moved into their new affordable home.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Holmes is May, and soon the world's sport will come to the world's borough with the fully electric, privately financed Etihad Stadium scheduled to be ready to welcome NYCFC for the 2027

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: MLS season and open to the world for the 2028 Olympics.

chapters.summary: Jung discusses the Hunts Point produce market redevelopment as a fully electric facility delivering healthy food to working families while addressing Bronx environmental justice. He closes with the South Brooklyn Marine Terminal as the nation's largest offshore wind hub, 98% complete, with 2,500 union workers and first power expected by year-end.
chapters.title: Jung on Hunts Point market and South Brooklyn Marine Terminal offshore wind hub

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: This is a model of green neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And last December, we took another major step forward in reimagining what the future of our city can be.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We advanced the redevelopment of the Hunts Point produce market, an investment in the people who keep the city running every single day.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: This transformation will create a first of its kind fully electric facility, one that delivers affordable, healthy food to working families while building a cleaner, more sustainable future for the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: For too long, communities in the Bronx have borne the burden of pollution and poor air quality, while receiving too little of the investments they deserve.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: This project is about changing that reality.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We know that we must build a green future where the benefits are shared, where clean energy means cleaner air in our neighborhoods, good paying jobs for our workers, and a stronger city for everyone.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And at the South Brooklyn Marine Terminal, we are building that future with the largest offshore wind hub in the nation.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: In the heart of our Sunset Park District, a critical focal point in the city's efforts to grow just green economy, more than 2,500 union workers have been revitalizing this port into the clean energy center of New York City.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: This is where our waterfront history meets our climate and innovation future.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Construction is 98% complete, and we expect first power to reach the terminal by the end of the year.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Building the green economy takes ambition and boldness.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Green Economy Action Plan is about choosing a future where cleaner air comes with better jobs, where climate resilience comes with economic security, where public investment creates public goods, and where the benefits of a greener city are shared by the many.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The green economy is not a distant promise.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: It is being built now on our waterfronts, in our neighborhoods, and by our working people.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We're proud to work in partnership with the City Council to improve the lives of our neighbors, expand opportunity, combat climate change, and ensure New York City remains a global hub of business, culture, and innovation.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I want to thank you for the opportunity to speak with you today, and we welcome any questions that you have.

chapters.summary: Douglas Perry, Executive Director of NYC Talent, introduces himself and Stephanie Cloakie. He describes the city's workforce ecosystem of 120+ programs across 28 agencies, the federal investment context under Biden's infrastructure laws that motivated the GEEP, and NYC Talent's role in aligning workforce strategy with employer demand.
chapters.title: Douglas Perry (NYC Talent) on workforce ecosystem and federal policy context

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: My name is Douglas Perry, and I'm the Executive Director of the New York City Office of Talent and Workforce Development, or NYC Talent.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I'm joined today by Stephanie Cloakie, the Executive Director of our industry partnership for the sustainable infrastructure sector.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Thank you to the members of the New York City Council Committees on Workforce Development and Economic Development, Workforce Development Chair Juan,

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: and Economic Development Chair Maloney for inviting us to testify today.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And thank you to our colleagues at EDC, Sam Jung and Michelle Lamberti, for your partnership.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: NYC Talent's mission is to ensure that the city's workforce system connects New Yorkers to good jobs and helps employers access local talent.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: The public workforce ecosystem in New York City is made up of over 120 programs administered by 28 different city agencies and their many contracted providers.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: This includes the city's core workforce agencies like the Department of Small Business Services, the Human Resources Administration, the Department of Youth and Community Development, the City University of New York, and New York City Public Schools.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: While each of these agencies focuses on its own core mission and service delivery, NYC Talent works across all partners so that the city's workforce development strategy align with job seeker needs, employer demand, and a changing economy.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Investments in sustainability and climate change resiliency have been building for decades, but in 2021 and 2022, former President Biden allocated the largest investment in climate action in U.S.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: History by signing the BIPAP.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: partisan infrastructure law, and the Inflation Reduction Act.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Between the two bills, billions of dollars were allocated to infrastructure projects and numerous tax incentives were developed to encourage electrification, electric vehicle use, clean and renewable energy generation, and energy storage.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: In the wake of these planned investments, EDC and NYC Talent developed the Green Economy Action Plan, which, as EDC described, outlined the current and projected size and scope of the green economy in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And offered actions city stakeholders could take to support the equitable growth of this sector.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Our role in the report's development focused on ensuring that all stakeholders, including our partners in government and in the private sector, are proactively thinking about workforce development as a tool to connect New Yorkers to opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: NYC Talent knows that workforce development services and systems constitute essential opportunity infrastructure that is needed to equitably achieve our climate and economic goals.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And just like physical infrastructure, workforce pathways are not built overnight.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We work closely with ADC to develop this report by informing recommendations, facilitating conversations with industry stakeholders, and helping to identify the good jobs that are key.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: To building toward a decarbonized future.

chapters.summary: Perry describes federal policy rollbacks including House Resolution 1, executive orders, and tariffs that rescinded EV tax incentives and accelerated wind and solar credit phase-outs. He then introduces REMAKE, NYC Talent's new industry partnership for sustainable infrastructure, bringing together stakeholders across manufacturing, construction, transportation, utilities, and water systems.
chapters.title: Perry on federal rollbacks and REMAKE industry partnership

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: The federal policy landscape has shifted considerably since the Green Economy Action Plan's original release in February 2024.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Reckless, cruel, and short-sighted policy changes, including House Resolution 1, executive orders, and tariffs, have rescinded tax incentive for electric vehicles, accelerated the phase-out of tax credits for wind and solar projects, reprioritized fossil fuels,

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: and created an atmosphere of economic uncertainty for workers, businesses, and other stakeholders active in the green economy and beyond.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Despite these headwinds, this administration and NYC Talent believe strongly in climate change and are committed to ensuring the growth of the green economy benefits all New Yorkers, particularly communities disproportionately impacted by environmental injustices.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: With this unwavering vision, since the report's release, NYC Talent has launched a new industry partnership focused on resilient infrastructure,

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: taken significant steps to increase equitable access to careers in the construction industry, supported green public sector pathways, and developed new opportunities for the city's young people to learn about and gain skills relevant to the green economy.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We also continue to support agencies across the public workforce system, as well as private partners, in aligning workforce strategies to meet the demand of the green economy.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: In 2025, NYC Talent launched a new industry partnership to expand and lead our office's work in the green economy, the Resiliency, Manufacturing, Industrial, and Infrastructure Council, or REMAKE for short.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: REMAKE works to support equitable growth within the sustainable infrastructure field by bringing together stakeholders across sectors including manufacturing,

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: industrial, construction, transportation, utilities, decarbonization, supply chain, waste and materials management, building operations, and water systems to coordinate workforce development strategies and solutions.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: As outlined in the plan and mentioned in EDC's testimony, some industries in the green economy will see the creation of entirely new jobs, while many others will adapt by changing the skills required within existing.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: occupations.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: For example, electricians install building electrification systems and EV charging infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: HVAC mechanics support heat pump deployment and energy efficiency retrofits.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Plumbers and pipe fitters work on high efficiency building systems and construction managers oversee projects that must meet evolving climate and energy performance requirements.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: To monitor these adaptations and understand ongoing employer demand, Remake engages businesses across New York City's green economy to better understand emerging hiring trends.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Remake has hosted seven industry roundtables with more than 35 businesses and continues to conduct site visits with small manufacturers to hear directly about recruitment and retention challenges.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We also actively engage climate tech startups as they begin planning for future workforce growth.

chapters.summary: Perry describes REMAKE's seven industry roundtables with 35+ businesses, a labor market intelligence webinar series on energy auditors and managers, and the PINC program placing nearly 1,500 participants into union or prevailing wage careers since 2024. He also covers the community hiring initiative with 275+ procurements valued at $14 billion.
chapters.title: Perry on labor market intelligence, PINC program, and community hiring

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: These engagements help Remake to provide guidance to businesses interested in developing on-the-job training opportunities and has informed the launch of a labor market intelligence webinar series designed to connect the workforce field directly with businesses.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We recently hosted our first webinar, which focused on labor market trends relating to certified energy auditors and certified energy managers.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: This session attracted representatives from training providers, CUNY, and city agencies, underscoring the importance of translating employer demand into actionable workforce development strategies.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: NYC Talent is also helping more New Yorkers enter fields that are critical to the development of a climate resilient future, including the building trades, facility managers, and more.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: The City's Pathways to Industrial and Construction Careers, or PINK, program, which is a

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: managed by HRA in partnership with our office, provides expansive supportive services, including funding for transportation, costs associated with starting a job like uniforms and tools, and driver's licenses, so that low-income individuals can successfully start and retain a job.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Since 2024, nearly 1,500 PING participants have been placed into union or prevailing wage careers in sectors including construction, transportation, and building services.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Another example of expanding equitable pathways into quality careers is our community hiring initiative, which leverages the city's purchasing power to connect city vendors to talent and job seekers to career opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Through community hiring, city agencies set workforce goals in their contracts for vendors to provide employment and apprenticeship opportunities to low-income individuals and those who live in low-income communities, including NYCHA housing.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Community hiring covers green economy-related investments, including construction and professional services.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: This initiative, which was made possible by state legislation, was operationalized by NYC Talent in 2025, and to date, more than 275 procurements have been released with community hiring goals, which represents $14 billion in projected contract value.

chapters.summary: Perry discusses Project Labor Agreements covering $9 billion in cumulative project value, the most capital construction work ever, with community hiring goals embedded. He notes $1 million in baseline funding for construction pre-apprenticeship starting in FY2025 secured with SBS.
chapters.title: Perry on Project Labor Agreements and pre-apprenticeship funding

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I also lead the city's negotiation for the project labor agreements, or PLAs, which are specific types of collective bargaining agreements that the city negotiates with the building trades for certain types of construction projects.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: PLAs are a great tool to ensure cost-effective, efficient, and safe capital project delivery while ensuring labor harmony, fair wages, and benefits for workers, and opportunities for other workforce development.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And efforts.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: The city signed new PLAs across 2024 and 2025 that cover a cumulative project value of approximately

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: only $9 billion, including many resilient infrastructure investments, like the construction of new parks and buildings, installation and renovation of wastewater filtration plants, and more.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: While the city has had PLAs since 2009, the recent PLAs cover the most amount of capital construction work ever and also include community hiring goals directly in the PLAs.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: This increases access to quality union careers for more New Yorkers, which will set them on a path to contribute to and benefit from the sector's green transition.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And to make these apprenticeship goals more equitable and impactful, NYC Talent worked with SBS to secure $1 million in baseline funding for construction pre-apprenticeship programming starting in fiscal year 2025.

chapters.summary: Perry covers NYC Cool Roofs program expansion with $550,000 in new state grant funding, DCAS fleet workforce EV safety training for 300+ city employees, youth apprenticeships funded by Robin Hood Foundation with a new Youth Apprentice Civil Service title, Future Ready HVAC and building decarbonization pathway at 11 high schools serving 400 students, and Inside Industrial career exploration tours for 350+ students.
chapters.title: Perry on Cool Roofs, DCAS fleet training, and youth apprenticeships

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Our partners at SBS also continue to support green workforce programming via its training in construction site safety, cable installation, and NYC cool roofs.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: NYC Talent has directly supported the expansion of NYC cool roofs, which provide New Yorkers with paid training and work experience installing energy-saving reflective rooftops.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: By steering investments from the New York State's Office of Just Energy Transition, more than $550,000 in new state grant funding from this office has gone to the cool roofs program.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We also partnered with the Department of Citywide Administrative Services to upskill the public sector fleet workforce.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: The first of its kind foundational training increases safety awareness for incumbent city employees working around electric vehicles and their high voltage systems, particularly for non-technicians and support personnel, and is on track to serve more than 300 city workers.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: NYC Talent is also preparing the city's next generation of public sector workers for the growth of the green economy through our expanding youth apprenticeships.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Funded by a grant from the Robin Hood Foundation, NYC Talent is working with DCAS and city agencies to leverage the city's new Youth Apprentice Civil Service title to expand access to in-demand green occupations at city agencies.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: NYC Talent's support for youth employment continues beyond public sector careers.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Leveraging our industry expertise, we contributed to New York City Public Schools'expansion of Future Ready, which now includes an HVAC and building decarbonization pathway.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: This pathway was launched in 2024 and expands career-connected learning opportunities in green economy sectors.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: This pathway is currently available at 11 high schools and serves nearly 400 students in grades 10 through 12.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: To date, New York City Public Schools has committed $6 million in capital funding to support their green economy workforce initiatives.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Additionally, NYC Talent launched Inside Industrial, a career exploration initiative that brings New York City public school students to employer worksites so that students can experience a career in the green economy and employers can build connections with New York City's talented young people.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: To date, NYC Talent has held 13 tours for more than 350 students across seven schools.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We recently partnered with DYCD to bring on cohorts of middle school students through their Exploring Futures program and are looking forward to identifying additional opportunities to grow and scale inside industrial.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: There is no doubt that the impacts of the federal cuts are significant and growth of some occupations with

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: in the green economy may be slowed, but the need for a more sustainable and climate resilient future is undeniable, and this administration is committed to meeting this challenge through actions, policies, and strategies that center working people.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We are proud of the city's progress to date, and we will continue to develop more equitable pathways into the careers that power the green economy's growth.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: By strengthening pathways into these industries now, we are ensuring that New Yorkers across the five boroughs can benefit from New York City's transition to a more climate resilient future.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Thank you, and we look forward to your questions.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks whether the Mamdani administration plans to continue pursuing the GEEP and which commitments will be funded in the upcoming budget. Jung responds that EDC views the plan as a generational commitment, citing the South Brooklyn Marine Terminal as spanning three administrations. He says current initiatives are funded through existing allocations and EDC will work with OMB for new initiatives.
chapters.title: Maloney asks about Mamdani admin commitment and budget funding for GEEP

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you so much for that testimony.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And thank you for the updates.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: It's great to hear that many steps are being taken to pursue the goals of the Green Energy Action, Economy Action Plan.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Just to start off, since this plan was first released, we've shifted

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: presidents.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: We've shifted presidents of EDC and we also have a new mayor.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So can you just speak to the Mondami's administration's goals and plans to continue to pursue the plan and if yes, which of the plans commitments are going to be funded in the upcoming budget?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, for the question.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: As you know, one of the core remits of the New York City Economic Development Corporation is to grow innovation-based sectors with a focus on equity.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And so to that end, we as EDC view our work more as generational investments and commitments.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And so although the plan was released under the previous mayor's administration, the Green Economy Action Plan really should be thought about as a generational and ongoing commitment.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Particularly given that the urgency of addressing climate crisis is only deepening.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I think a good example of this is the South Brooklyn Marine Terminal.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: In the de Blasio administration, that was when

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We put forward that site for solicitation that Equinor was then successful in enabling a port facility for.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: That construction started in the Adams administration, and that site will be completed in the Mamdani administration.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I will note, like I said in my testimony before, that project in particular has afforded 2,500 union workers to come to the site and work there.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: It will yield 200 assembly jobs for the assembly of the large wind turbine components and 60 permanent jobs for O&M, which are enduring and really well-paying.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We were able to also extract some community benefits from Equinor in particular, which includes a brand new learning center for offshore wind, which is called Windscape, located at Industry City.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We also extracted $5 million of investments for local workforce and business development organizations, including Uprose.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And they funded the first three years of the offshore wind innovation hub to support our innovation work in the offshore wind sector.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: So that project really exemplifies ADC's commitment to working at the intersection of climate and economic justice, particularly given that that project will facilitate 810 megawatts of clean energy into New York City's grid,

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: fairly alleviating our fossil fuel grid-based system here.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And just speaking towards the upcoming budget and the funding commitments, do we expect any shifts or what components of the plan are going to be supported?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Yeah, currently many of our initiatives are funded through existing allocations.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We'll work closely with OMB in the JAM plan process for any resources that we need for net new initiatives, but generally we are covered for those initiatives in the Jeep as far as city funding is concerned.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks if the Mamdani administration's framing of green economy around climate and labor policy integration has shifted the plan's focus. Jung says EDC takes an 'all of the above' approach supporting both union and non-union jobs, and welcomes the emphasis on union jobs. Perry adds the PINC program has placed nearly 1,500 people into union pathways.
chapters.title: Maloney asks about climate-labor policy integration shift

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: The Mondami administration has framed the green economy around the integration of climate and labor policy.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: My question is if that's shifted any of the focus of the green economy action plan or the framing in any of the strategies outlined in 2024.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Yeah, in terms of workforce development, EDC takes the approach, an all of the above approach.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We think supporting the creation of good quality jobs is the imperative, which includes union jobs and non-union jobs.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And so we welcome the Mamdani administration's emphasis on union jobs and we'll do all that we can to continue our good work to enable union jobs, particularly at construction sites.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: But yeah, we'll continue our all of the above strategy for workforce development.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Do you have any comments?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And I will just note the pathways to industrial and construction.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: careers program that I mentioned has placed nearly 1,500 people into union and prevailing wage careers.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So that's a really exciting opportunity, not just to connect people to any old job, but to union pathways, which we know are the pathway to the middle class.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Great.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks for the current estimate of green jobs and growth rate. Jung gives an economic snapshot showing unemployment down to 5.4%, 44,500 private sector jobs added over the past year. He explains the GEEP defined the green economy across eight sectors and 21 subsectors, and that the 400,000 projection is contingent on four factors. He notes 70% are transitioning jobs that don't lend themselves to year-by-year tracking.
chapters.title: Maloney asks about current green jobs estimate; Jung gives economic snapshot and methodology

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Digging into the numbers, the plan set the goal of 400,000 jobs up from 130 at the release.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So what is the current estimate of green jobs in the city today?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And what is the growth rate that you've seen?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair, for the question.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I wanted to first start by kind of emphasizing some key takeaways from today's economic snapshot that the EDC just released.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Three key takeaways there.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Unemployment is down for the third straight month to 5.4%.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: New York City has added 44,500 private sector jobs over the past year, a 1.1 increase, which is faster than the national growth of 0.5% over the same period,

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: and that the broader New York City metro area added about 17,300 jobs in May and leads all U.S.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Metros with

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: 48,600 jobs added over the past year.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: So I think those stats really underscore the importance of EDC's work to diversify the economy and give some good light about the state of our local economy in particular now.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Like I mentioned before, the Just Transition creates a big opportunity for us

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: to advance economic justice and create economic opportunities for everyday New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: To seize the economic opportunities that it affords, we, through the Green Economy Action Plan, did a couple of things.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The first one was to define what the green economy actually is.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: It may be surprising, but there isn't a national consensus on what constitutes the green economy, so we defined that.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Consists of eight sectors and 21 subsectors.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And relatedly, we use a Jeep to size and forecast what might be the potential for New York City to host

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: 400,000 green jobs by 2040, which I will say is not a commitment, but a projection and a forecast.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Could you repeat the number?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I'm sorry?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Could you repeat the number?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Oh, you said that.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Yeah, that's right.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And that 400,000 jobs is contingent on four key factors.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: One is local, state, and federal climate policies.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The second is public investments, particularly from the federal government.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And then three, private investments, and then four, shifting consumer preferences.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And so as you stated in your opening remarks, when we released the plan, there was 133,000 green jobs.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And 70% of those jobs are transitioning into the green economy.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I mentioned that in particular because the data does not lend itself to year by year

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: tracking.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: It is difficult, for example, to understand when a financing consultant will do more green economy work.

chapters.summary: Jung provides specific numbers: 68,000 jobs in clean energy occupations in 2021 growing to 75,000 by 2024. In 21 focus occupations largely in building efficiency, growth was 8.6%. He mentions Willets Point generating 14,000+ jobs, the Batworks 30,000 sq ft facility, Bronx Green Job Center, and $10 million CUNY investment.
chapters.title: Jung provides specific clean energy job growth numbers and capital projects

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And so the nature of the projections takes that into account.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We have a methodology in place, but given the timeframe that we've had, we can't replicate that.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: But I will note that in the clean energy generation, transmission, distribution, and storage and energy efficiency

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: occupations for which we have numbers, there were about 68,000 jobs in those occupations in 2021, and now there are about 75,000

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: as of 2024, which is the latest data set that we have access to currently.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Within the...

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Occupations that we discuss in the Green Economy Action Plan, there's 21 focus occupations.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Those are jobs largely within the building efficiency and electrification space, where we feel like there are lower barriers to entry for everyday New Yorkers that pay family sustaining wages of $31 per hour and are largely accessible.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We've seen growth from 2021 to 2024 at a rate of 8.6%, so we see promising job growth figures there.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Like I mentioned in my testimony, we see this as generational work.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The jobs won't be created overnight.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We as EDC are confident that we will continue to see job growth and that green economy itself, given the nature of the crisis, will only continue to heighten.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: the need to be addressed, thereby creating economic opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I will say that our work hasn't stopped.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Like I mentioned in my remarks, we have various capital projects like the Willits Point development, which includes all electric soccer stadium, which in totality will generate more than 14,000 jobs.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We've invested in different workforce facilities all across the city, which I can speak to a little bit more later, but which includes a 30,000 square foot workforce development facility at Batworks in the Brooklyn Army Terminal, the Bronx Green Job Center,

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: and we made $10 million investment into the community central.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: campuses.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And so we're happy to share that news and continue the good work ahead.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I'm going to pass to Chair Juan.

chapters.summary: Won asks how Local Law 97 compliance will proceed without federal, state, or city funding and how it affects promised green jobs. Jung says DOB reports 90% compliance with the first milestone and EDC supplements with workforce and business development support.
chapters.title: Won asks about Local Law 97 compliance and green jobs impact

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Yeah, I have some follow-up questions.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So for local law 97 compliance compared to the five bars, can you help me understand how you guys are still going to– right now there's no funding from the federal, state, or city government for local law 97 implications or implementation.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So how does this affect the green jobs that we were promised, and what is the plan there?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Thank you for the question, Chair Juan.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: You know, as we noted in our testimony earlier, buildings are one of the biggest emitters of carbon dioxide and also creates the biggest opportunity for jobs in the green economy.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And so we have a shared understanding of the importance of enabling Local Law 97 implementation.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We've heard from our agency partners that they're making great strides.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: DOB in particular has shared that there's been over 90% compliance with their first milestone for private building owners.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I will say that DOB is the implementation and enforcement agency.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We as EDC supplement their efforts by supporting on workforce development and business development.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So how many jobs are being created by Local Law 97 implementation?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Like I mentioned before, in the 21 focus occupations that we've seen, we've seen about 8.6% growth.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: A lot of those are from the building occupations.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So how many jobs is 8.6%?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I don't have that exact figure and I don't want to misspeak, so I'm happy to come back.

chapters.summary: Won asks how many jobs LL97 is creating and presses for a breakdown of the 400,000 projection. Jung cites 8.6% growth in 21 focus occupations but cannot provide an exact job count. Won asks the team to look up the number and demands a breakdown of how the 400,000 forecast was constructed.
chapters.title: Won asks for breakdown of 400K jobs projection

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Okay, can your team look it up right now so that we can understand how many jobs that is?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: And can you help me break down the 400,000 jobs?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So you're saying that it's going to be 400,000 jobs by 2040, and that's up from 133,000.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Even if you were saying that right now, you can't tell me and the other chair how many jobs that is going to be year to year.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Help me understand what...

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Your vision was when you set the number 400,000 for the next four years.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So what for the next, I guess, until 2040.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So how are you guys looking to increase the jobs by 2040?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: What is the breakdown that you made that forecast?

chapters.summary: Perry explains the 400,000 was a projection based on information at the time including enormous federal investments that did not follow through. Won presses for which funding levels and jobs-per-funding estimates were used, arguing the projection should be recalculated given current realities.
chapters.title: Won asks how 400K forecast was calculated

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Thanks for the question, Chair Juan.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Like I said before, there are certain data limitations given the availability of data sets.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So we're not able to...

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Right, but I'm saying why did you guys forecast 400,000?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: How did you get to that number is what I'm asking you.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: The 400,000 number was a projection based on the information at the time the report was developed, which included the enormous amount of investments from the federal government.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Unfortunately, that investment, all of that investment did not follow through.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Let's break down that investment.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So can you help me understand for which level of funding and how many jobs per funding you are estimating for so that we got to the 400,000?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
