utterances.text: Because then we should recalculate how many jobs we're really expecting by 2040 and break that down.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Because we're trying to become, we want to be realistic here.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So we want to understand what we're actually dealing with here.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: We can't just be projecting fake numbers to people with fake hopes.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I think the point is well taken, Chair Juan.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I will underscore what I said before, that that 400 job number is a projection based on four different factors, local, state, federal policy, public investments, private investments.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Which policies, how much money are we talking about?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: We can break down the 400,000 jobs.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We're unable to do that at that point, at this point, because the methodology doesn't lend itself to a clear answer.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Okay, so then help me understand how you got to the 400,000 to begin with.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: So we took a wide...

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Wide spreading methodology that incorporates analysis of the various four factors that I just mentioned to you before and did

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: analysis that accounted for the different peaks and valleys of different industry growth cycles, public policy milestones, etc.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Which industries and which cycles?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: To come up with a cumulative figure that is 400,000 jobs by 2040.

chapters.summary: Won asks which specific industries and growth cycles were used in the forecast methodology, saying the public deserves to know so they can calculate a realistic number. Perry says it's too early to know whether the city is on track, suggesting 2030 as a benchmark year to reassess, given the shift from Biden to Trump administration policies.
chapters.title: Won asks which industries and cycles drove 400K projection

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So which industries and which cycles are you referring to?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: The public deserves to know how you forecast this for the 400,000 so that we can make up for the delta and come to a realistic number of what that actually will be for job creation with our realities today.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Definitely appreciate the question, Chair One.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I want to step back a little bit because I think we share both your goals and concerns and want to be transparent with the public about like where our numbers are, where we're heading.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I think what my colleagues are alluding to, and I'll try to be like a little clear about it, is that it clearly was a projection, right?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: The 400,000 was a projection based on what we knew at the time.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: That said, I think it's, from our perspective, it still feels a little too early to know whether or not we are on track.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I understand the desire.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: for concrete numbers every year, the way that the projections were based was really over like longer cycles.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So while we are currently, like when the plan rolled out, obviously we were under the Biden administration, who had like really fulsome investments in many of these sectors.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I think we have definitely seen a rollback with the Trump administration.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so,

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We need to wait a little bit longer, I think, to see what future administrative policies may look like.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: That said, we are continuing to move our pillars of the plan forward.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And I think it's important to note that many of the things that were in the plan, I know we've said this before, and I know when I first read the plan, I wanted to understand it a little bit better, that many of the jobs are transitioning, right?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So like they're jobs that currently exist.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So that's your HVAC person who's doing something that's like not.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: My colleagues will help me on the correct terminology, but they're doing the HVAC work, but they're doing it in ways that don't necessarily employ green technology or the latest green practices.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Those jobs are not complete.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Those are transition.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We consider those transitioning jobs, right?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So there's like a subset of jobs that will transition who are jobs that exist today.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: It's not 400,000 net new.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: It's a projection of 400,000 green jobs.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So some of those will be net new and some of those will be transitioning.

chapters.summary: Won asks what percentage of the 400,000 are transition jobs. Perry confirms 70% are transitioning existing jobs and 30% are net new. Won asks when the forecast will be corrected given federal and state cuts, and Perry says the plan recommends looking to 2030, noting it's too early just two years in.
chapters.title: Won asks about transition jobs percentage and forecast correction timeline

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So what percentage of the 400,000 are transition jobs?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: That we may have to get back.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: 70% are transitioning jobs.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So 70% out of 400,000 are existing transition jobs.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: I think that's a really important clarification for the public to know.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I think.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: To be clear, I think that was clear in the Green Economy Action Plan, and it is true that 70% of those jobs are transitioning jobs, with the additional 30% being net new.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So when are you going to make the correction?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Because it's two years into the administration now that you haven't made the correction in the forecast for $400,000.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I think the plan forecasts $400,000, and in the plan it makes reference to the fact that those are both net new and transitioning jobs.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So that is clear in the plan.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: When are you going to make a correction for your forecast, knowing that there's federal and state cuts to all of the green new jobs?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I think we have other, there's a benchmark in there, I think, where we recommend looking to 2030.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I think we're two years in.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I think in terms of understanding what the real effects are within the cycle will take a little bit of time.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so I'd say, like, as we get closer to the 2030 period, it might be a more realistic time to look back to see, like, where are we in terms of the economic cycle?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Where are we within terms of do we still have an administration like the one we have currently, or have we shifted back to an admin that's closer aligned with the Biden admin that is really prioritizing the work we're doing around green economy?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So when is the next time?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So are you saying that we're not going to get an update for the public until 2030 for the job forecast?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I think what I can say is that because the 400,000 was a projection and the methodology, as my colleagues alluded to, is difficult, that we would, the most appropriate time to

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Perhaps revisit, and I would have to consult with my colleagues, would be closer to that 2030 date, but not just two years into the plan.

chapters.summary: Won asks for a clear breakdown of the $600 million allocated for green career pathways. Perry clarifies the $600 million reflects FY24 funding for the broader Youth Action Plan, not exclusively green economy. Won presses for how much is specifically for green workforce development, and Perry says they would need to work with partner agencies to break it down.
chapters.title: Won asks for $600M green career pathways breakdown

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Can you give us a clear breakdown?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Because through the both testimonies from Talent and from EDC, we heard snippets of small amounts of money under $10 million spent here, spent there.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Can you give us a clear breakdown of the $600 million that has been allocated for the Green Economy Action Plan and where that money is going?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So to clarify, the $600 million, as you mentioned earlier, is a reflection of the investment from the city, state, federal, and private funding for the implementation of the Youth Young Adult Action Plan.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And that was released in December 2023 and was reflective of the FY24 funding.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Now, that funding was to support all sorts of youth workforce development programming, which include, but is not exclusively limited to, investments in the green economy.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So that $600 million was tied to youth workforce, but that includes, say, like summer youth employment program.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So what is the breakdown of the $600 million that is specifically for green workforce development?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So we don't have the specific numbers because some of the programming, like SYEP, for example, right, for that fiscal year was over $200 million.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And SYEP, we just worked with DYCD and the painters DC9 to have a SYEP program.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so DC9 will be hosting.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: 30 different young people this summer.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so that's one example where we're having young people get exposure to and learn from a job that is contributing to the green economy.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: But because it's part of SYEP, we don't have that specific.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Okay, so with all of those different overlaps that you have, how much of the 600 million is for green new jobs?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We would have to get back to you on some of the specifics if we can break it down to that.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Okay, can someone on your team please do that right now?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: I'm going to pass it back to Chair Maloney.

chapters.summary: Maloney echoes the need for revised numbers, then asks about SBMT construction status and jobs created. Jung explains the federal, state, and local government roles in offshore wind, confirms SBMT is 98% complete, and details lease protections including a construction guarantee, lease guarantee through 2054, a $20 million penalty clause, and reimbursement-based city capital.
chapters.title: Maloney asks about SBMT status and jobs; Jung explains jurisdiction and lease protections

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Wan.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Echoing the sentiment, the original report had four growth drivers that have considerably shifted since the original.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Report was published, public policy and targets, citing state and federal laws, government spending and incentives, primarily citing federal funding that was directed towards

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: the green economy, and then consumer preferences, which have also probably shifted in an increasingly

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: in an affordability crisis.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So echo Chair Wan's sentiment of needing to revise the numbers to be more realistic when the Center for the Urban Future was citing that there's been 2,000 net new green jobs in

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: 2023 and that delta of things for

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: asking that line of questioning.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I wanted to

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Jump over to the South Brooklyn Marine Terminal and ask what the status is of the current construction at the terminal, how many jobs have been created there.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Happy to do so.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I think the top lines that I'll say about SBMT and Empire Wind 1 and the future pipelines include, one, the city's interests are protected at the South Brooklyn Marine Terminal.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Empire Wind 1 has faced federal obstruction, but is unlikely to face them in the future.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And then three, the future pipeline of new offshore wind projects is challenged.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: For context there, I'll just briefly go over the different roles that each level of government play with an offshore wind first.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: At the federal level, they are responsible for giving site control to offshore wind developers in what they call wind lease areas in federal waters, and also to permit the offshore construction.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: So Empire Wind 1 as an offshore wind project is subject to federal jurisdiction in that regard.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: New York State in particular creates a market for offshore wind.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: They solicit and procure for offshore wind renewable energy credits, which enable offshore wind developers to get the financing that they need to finance the overall onshore and offshore projects.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The local government provides site and infrastructure to different onshore components of the offshore wind farm.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: which includes the South Brooklyn Marine Terminal.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: So you just wanted to get that clear from the start.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: At the South Brooklyn Marine Terminal, like I mentioned before, it's 98% complete.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Just to speak to the ways in which we protected the city's interest through the lease there, we had a construction guarantee, first and foremost.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We wanted to ensure that if there was going to be federal obstruction, that Equinor and Skanska don't walk away from the project and that we as a city are made whole with a complete

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: world-class Osher Windport, which we are on track to be completed very soon.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We also have a lease guarantee through the term of Equinor's sublease, which is from 2026 to 2054.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: So they ultimately are on the hook for rent payments to the city.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And there is a $20 million penalty if they renege on their lease.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And then we have invested into South Brooklyn Marine Terminal with our city capital.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: That, as you probably know, is reimbursement-based.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And so we will not pay out those resources until the work is completed, given that they, as Equinor, have to pay out of pocket first, and then we will repay once that is.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: completed.

chapters.summary: Jung provides job numbers: 2,500 unique union workers on site, 200 assembly jobs, and 60 permanent O&M jobs. He then addresses the future offshore wind pipeline, noting federal obstruction has halted new permitting and procurements, but EDC is working with NYSERDA on pre-development RFPs and supporting projects like CHPE bringing 1,250 megawatts of Canadian hydropower.
chapters.title: Jung provides SBMT job numbers and future offshore wind pipeline challenges

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: In terms of the discrete job question that you asked, like I mentioned before, there's been over 2,500 unique union workers on site working.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: There will be 200 workers that will assemble the large wind turbine components, and then there will be 60 permanent jobs for operations and maintenance for the

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: offshore portion of Empire One's maintenance.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I think I'll just speak a little bit to the future pipeline.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We know that given federal obstruction, there's been aggressive tactics that have been implemented that have, one, halted all new permitting and

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: procurements for federal wind lease areas.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: So we do see the future being challenged there, but we are working hand in glove with New York State and NYSERDA, which still has a nine gigawatt goal of offshore wind

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: to ensure that we're doing all that we can to sustain the offshore wind development industry if and as a new federal administration that's friendlier to offshore wind comes into place.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: For example, we've been supporting NYSERDA on what they call a pre-development RFP.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: So the state is looking to the broad industry and local government partners for ideas on how to support pre-development activities that offshore wind developers will need so that they can jumpstart

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: when there is a friendly administration in place.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I'll close by saying that moving forward, we see offshore wind as a critical component of our clean energy future, but that we're also taking an all of the above approach to ensuring a just transition

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: to renewables, which includes supporting projects like CHIPPE, the Champlain Hudson Power Express, which we supported with some incentives there, that is bringing

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: 1,250 megawatts of clean hydropower from Canada to New York City.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks about contingency plans if the federal court reverses and about the reduced scope from three offshore projects to one 810 MW project. Jung clarifies the stop work orders never affected SBMT construction given its onshore nature. On ROI, he emphasizes the lease guarantee ensures Equinor pays rent and EDC is actively helping find other maritime uses for the site.
chapters.title: Maloney asks about SBMT contingency plan and reduced scope ROI

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And just to clarify, what is the contingency plan if the injunction is lifted or if federal court, the appeals process, if they prevail?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And if we're at 98% complete, what's the timeline?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Yeah, I'll clarify.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Just given, like I mentioned before, the jurisdiction of the federal government only really pertains to federal offshore construction.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: When there were two stop work orders that did not stop construction at SBMT both times, we have never had to comply with the stop work order given its onshore nature and given that it's city land.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: To that line of questioning, the 2022 agreement was justified as the staging and assembly hub because there were three offshore projects that were in the pipeline, Empire Wind 1, Empire Wind 2,

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: and Beacon Wind 1, which together would have generated more than 3,000 megawatts.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Empire Wind 2 was canceled.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Beacon Wind has no active construction, and the terminal now has an 810 megawatt project.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So how does the economic case for the city's investment in the terminal hold when the...

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: scope of work has reduced down to one project and have you reassess the return on investment expected?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Yeah, I think that's where our lease provision that ensures a guarantee of rent is really important here.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We as EDC are of course supportive of Equinor finding multiple uses to ensure that they are able to meet the rent.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: But that onus is on them to ensure that they get subtenants or activate the site with uses that will generate that income to provide the city with rent.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I will note that we have actively partnered with Equinor to amplify the South Brooklyn Marine Terminal for other maritime dependent uses, which could include the storage and transportation of construction materials, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And so we're actively at the table trying to shape and ensure that they are successful.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: But ultimately, I will underscore that it is their responsibility.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: To give the city rent payments.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And what is the excess capacity at the site being used for?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Currently there is no excess capacity in terms of staging and assembly given that Empire Wind 1 is gonna be using the totality of that site.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Like I mentioned before, I think there's a,

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I'll have to get back to you on the discrete acreage, but we are actively supporting Equinor to find other uses for that site.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks about the Empire Wind power price increase from $118 to $155 per megawatt hour and who bears the cost. Jung says the cost is shared across all New Yorkers at $1-2 per month on electricity rates, with NYSERDA's evaluation criteria being 70% price, 20% local content, 10% feasibility. Perry adds that SBMT is the largest infrastructure investment in 50 years and the city remains bullish on wind long-term.
chapters.title: Maloney asks about Empire Wind price increase; Perry on SBMT economic importance

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: One other question.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: In June 2024, the state locked in the price for Empire Wind 1's power at $155 per megawatt hour, up from $118 that was proposed in the original award.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Who bears the cost of that?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: How much of the city's investment was premised on the other two sites preceding?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The power purchase agreement between NYSERDA and Equinor is for their one project, Empire Wind 1.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: That will cause, that cost burden is shared across all New Yorkers, and it will increase their monthly electricity rates about a dollar to two dollars.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And so that was a prime consideration for NYSERDA in their OREC solicitation.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Their evaluation criteria was 70% price, 20% what we call local content, meaning the economic impact for talent and workers and businesses across the state, and then 10% on feasibility.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: So we've worked hand in glove to ensure that the state knows that affordability, particularly energy burden, is addressed through any OREC solicitations, and so we're happy that they have minimized the costs.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Through the competitive procurement.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And I think, Chair Maloney, because you just circled back a little bit to the economic importance of the site and the city's return on investment with respect to the site.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: irrespective of where we are right now, this was the largest infrastructure investment in the city in really like 50 years.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so while there are current federal headwinds with respect to offshore wind, our current president, for his own reasons, doesn't seem to like a source of power that we think is a great way to fuel like transition.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: This infrastructure is going to be in place for the next like 50 plus years.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so we look forward to a future in which like wind will be a significant part of the city's infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And it may not happen today, but it will happen in the future.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: That said, I think as my colleague had alluded to, the site is 100% being used right now.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And that is anticipated for the next couple of years.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Like, so we are fully functional using that site for offshore wind more than like supporting the investment the city's made.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And one of my colleagues back at the office wanted me to like also report that we have the largest crane in the United States at this site.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So it is a huge infrastructure investment.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: It is something that we are looking forward to in the city.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And while I understand what the federal administration has said about wind, we are bullish on wind in the long term and think it will fully support the city.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I'm now going to pass to my colleague, Councilmember Shanel Thomas-Henry.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Thomas-Henry asks how NYC Talent tracks community hiring goals. Perry explains a digital labor compliance tool is being implemented for construction contracts to collect workforce data, while professional services contracts use Workforce One and HRA Business Link for tracking.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry asks about community hiring tracking system

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: I'll begin with NYC Talent, your community hiring initiative program.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: I see that you mentioned that workforce goals have been added into contracts.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: How are you tracking those hiring goals currently?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Yeah, so as I mentioned in the testimony, we have over 275 procurements out on the street that's valued at over $14 billion.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so not all of those procurements have yet resulted in contracts.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I have to go through the procurement process.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: But we do have a handful of contracts that have come online.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so in terms of tracking, it depends actually on the type of contract it is.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So for construction contracts, we're implementing a digital labor compliance tool that will collect workforce data, being able to understand who's working on those projects, how much they're being paid, the trade classifications, where they're from.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: As well as the progress towards the community hiring goals.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: For other types of contracts, like the professional services side, human services side, we are working with the city's Workforce One system and our partners at HRA Business Link and others to

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: be able to collect and track that data directly from the contractors who have those goals.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so they will be submitting that information to the city.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So this digital labor tool will be something in-house to your...

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Agency and the contractor would be responsible for sending you these?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: It will be a citywide used tool for all capital construction agencies.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So all the construction agencies that are doing construction right now, there's a paper-based process.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We're helping digitize that process.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so city vendors who are working with, say, DDC or parks or DEP, transportation, those contractors will use this tool.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so the information on it will allow us, our office, to track community hiring and workforce commitments, as well as allow city agencies to conduct more robust prevailing wage oversight and payment processing.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks how low-income communities are identified for community hiring targeting. Perry explains it's rooted in state legislation targeting zip codes where 15%+ of the population is below federal poverty guidelines, plus NYCHA housing, and the city publishes a list of qualifying zip codes.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry asks about identifying low-income communities for community hiring

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: And how are you identifying which low-income communities or residents for these contractors to target?

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Are you doing it by zip code, project area?

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: How is that working?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Yeah, so community hiring is rooted in state legislation.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so the state legislation has pretty prescriptive requirements.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So for a construction contract, it's zip codes in which 15% or more of the population is below the federal poverty guidelines.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so we publish a list of all of those zip codes.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: It also includes NYCHA housing as well.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So the goals on construction projects are based on those low-income regions, both on the zip code level as well as NYCHA housing.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks where the 11 schools with the Future Ready HVAC and building decarbonization pathway are located and how many per borough. Perry says he would need to get back with the specific school list.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry asks about youth pathway school locations by borough

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: With the Youth Pathways to a Green Economy, you said that there are 11 schools in the program.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Where are those schools located and how many per borough?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And sorry, the last part of that question.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: How many per borough?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Per borough.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I would have, I know that the,

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: New York City Public Schools Future Ready, the HVAC and building decarbonization pathway.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: It is offered at 11 schools, serving over 400 students.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We would have to get back to you on the specific list of schools that it's at, but there's 11 schools in total using that pathway.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks how many of the five planned green collar training facilities (one per borough) are currently open. Jung provides status: Brooklyn Batworks (30,000 sq ft, late 2028), Bronx Fordham Green Job Center (12,000 sq ft, end of year), Manhattan Solar One (6,500 sq ft, already open), Queens Queensborough Community College (timeline TBD), Staten Island College of Staten Island (timeline TBD).
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry asks about green collar training facilities status in each borough

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Green collar workforce training facilities.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: The plan said that there will be five, one in each borough.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: How many of those are currently open?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I'm happy to give an overview of those facilities, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The first one is in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The largest workforce development training facility across the five boroughs will be at Batworks.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: That's going to be 30,000 square feet.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We're going to be opening that in late 2028.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: In the Bronx, we're happy to invest $3 million into Fordham's Bronx Green Job Center.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: That's going to be 12,000 square feet and opened by the end of the year.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: In Manhattan, the Solon One Environmental Education Center in Midtown East, that's 6,500 square feet, that is already open.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And then, like I mentioned before, we collectively have made a $10 million investment into various CUNY campuses for green workforce development facilities, which includes one in Queens at Queensborough Community College for an energy education center house,

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: and one in Staten Island at the College of Staten Island for a sustainable business.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Finance and Entrepreneurship Center.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We're actively working with CUNY's capital team in particular to understand the

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: timelines for when they will be open and happy to get you that information once it's clear.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks how many MWBE businesses are currently qualified to participate in green initiatives. Perry does not have the number and says he will need to get back with the information.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry asks about MWBE participation in green initiatives

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Okay, in the interest of time, I'm going to skip to the MWBE business initiatives.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Do you have the number of MWBE businesses that are currently qualified in participating in these green opportunities?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Thank you for that question, Council Member.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I think we'll need to get back to you with that number, but can you just repeat the question again just in case I'm able to answer it?

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: How many MWB businesses are there in the city that are currently qualified to participate in these green initiatives?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: That is not information that I know right now, but happy to bring that back into work with the administration and focus at EDC.

chapters.summary: Thomas-Henry asks about mentor-protégé programs to help MWBEs build capacity for green economy work. Jung describes Construct NYC, EDC's MWBE construction capacity building program with designed on-ramps to EDC procurements, and Waterfront Pathways, funded by Equinor to help MWBEs enter offshore wind and waterfront construction.
chapters.title: Thomas-Henry asks about MWBE mentor-protégé capacity building programs

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Max, one more question.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Just to piggyback on that, is there anything in the plan to help these MWBEs, like any mentor protege programs to help them build capacity?

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: So if they're not currently...

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Capable of performing this that they will be?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Yeah, thanks for council member for the question.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We have a shared value there supporting MWPE's access opportunities within the green economy.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We have longstanding programs called Construct NYC and a newer program called Waterfront Pathways, both which we run in-house at EDC.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Construct NYC is our premier best-in-class MWPE construction business technical assistance and capacity building program.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We're proud to say that we've graduated multiple cohorts.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We have designed on-ramps onto EDC projects in particular for those companies to ensure that once they get those skills, they're able to get first offers on any EDC

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: procurements that we put out to bid, particularly for construction work.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Waterfront Pathways was also invested by Equinor.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: It was a program that Equinor invested in as well.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: That program was specifically created to address the disparities in waterfront construction and services work to enable MWPEs to enter offshore wind, but also to grow their capacities.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And contracting opportunities in waterfront construction, just given the nature of how many assets are on our 520 miles of coastline.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: So we're happy to follow up with more information on those two programs.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: They've been running very well, and kudos to our EDC and WPPE team for running those.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Thank you to both chairs.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Council member Hanif for your questions.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Hanif asks how the city ensures people with disabilities are included in the green economy vision and whether there are specific goals or benchmarks. Jung says the Windscape learning center was developed with disability advocates for accessible materials, but there are no codified goals. Perry notes NYC Talent houses the Center for Workplace Accessibility and Inclusion but does not currently slice green economy data by disability.
chapters.title: Hanif asks about disability inclusion in Green Economy Action Plan

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Hi, thanks for being here this afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I'll start with some questions about the inclusion of people with disabilities in the Green Economy Action Plan.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: The plan emphasizes creating an equitable workforce.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: How has the city ensured that people with disabilities are included in that vision?

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And are there any specific goals or benchmarks for their participation in green economy jobs?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Thanks, Councilmember, for the question.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I'm happy to answer first and pass it to my colleague.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: As I mentioned before, we're pretty bullish about enabling workers to enter into good paying jobs in the green economy.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: That starts with career awareness.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And so the education center that we built in partnership with Econor that is at Industry City was developed in partnership with disability advocates to ensure that all of the materials are accessible

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: to a broad variety of individuals, both across language, but also across ability.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: But that doesn't cover...

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: The job part.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And so we don't have any codified goals for that, but it is part and parcel of our approach to inclusive and equitable workforce development.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: But I'll pass it to my colleague.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: If there aren't benchmarks, how do you know that this is equitable?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So, like my colleague said, we don't have specific benchmarks tied to goals for jobs for people with disabilities, but at NYC Talent, we're home to the Center for Workplace Accessibility and Inclusion, and the way that we think about

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: workforce accessibility and inclusion is making sure that all types of jobs are available and accessible to people with disabilities, and so we work across all types of sectors, and

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: particularly with the green economy, right, there's a broad type of, as was noted earlier in the testimony and in the report itself, a broad spectrum of jobs that contribute to the green economy.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so part of the work of our team at QI is to work with our partners across the city, whether that's the city agencies and partners external, to make sure that there's opportunities open for people with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: But at this moment, you are not evaluating whether people with disabilities have been connected to a job.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We're tracking, we actually do track towards connecting people with disabilities towards jobs.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We have yet to slice that specifically in the green economy, but we're happy to take that back and happy to also work with you, council member, on ways that we can better track that.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: But we do work towards tracking across the various city programming and connecting people with disabilities to jobs.

chapters.summary: Hanif raises economic insecurity for people with disabilities and asks whether MOPED (Mayor's Office for People with Disabilities) has been involved in green economy planning. Perry says NYC Talent works closely with MOPED across all disability work but is unsure of their specific involvement in the GEEP development. Hanif recommends MOPED be brought in.
chapters.title: Hanif on MOPED involvement and economic insecurity for people with disabilities

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I mean, with all the federal cuts, economic insecurity is...

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: It continues to be on the rise for people with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And our...

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Our city has opportunities that should be reaching, as is said in the vision of the plan, to people with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: And then has MOPED, Mayor's Office for People with Disabilities, played a role in advising around

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: job circulation or engagement with people with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So our office works very closely with the mayor's office of people with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: But specifically on green economy.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I would have to get back to you on their specific involvement with the development of the Green Economy Action Plan.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I mean, it's okay if you don't.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I'm just trying to know.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Yeah, yeah, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I'm not sure specifically about their involvement with the development of the plan, but we work very closely with them across all of our work tied to people with disabilities.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We also work very closely with the team at EDC, and the city collaborates quite a bit on that.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Yeah, I would recommend that a moped be brought in.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I think this is certainly an area that we should have far more clarity around.

chapters.summary: Hanif asks how many New Yorkers have been connected to green economy occupations against the 12,000 target. Perry says approximately 1,500 through PINC, 350+ through pre-apprenticeship, 17 youth apprentices at city agencies. Hanif notes these numbers are nowhere near 12,000 and asks how the gap will be closed by 2040.
chapters.title: Hanif asks about 12K training target, current placements, and gap

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: The plan,

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: shares that there is a priority focus occupations in construction and installation, engineering and architecture, management and business,

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: with a target of 12,000 New Yorkers to green economy occupations, how many have been connected to date?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So we at Talent work across the entire system.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: As I mentioned in the testimony, we had about approximately 1,500 people through the PINK program connected to union and prevailing wage jobs.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We also work very closely with our colleagues at SBS who have funding for pre-apprenticeship programming.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And I think to date, over 350 people since the release of the plan or since FY24 have been connected to pre-apprenticeship.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Are these numbers available publicly?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We've reported on the, we just celebrated an exciting milestone of 1,300 for pink, and so we've reported on that publicly through that press release we just issued.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: It's now closer to 1,500.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Some of the other numbers through community hiring, for example, those numbers will be reported publicly.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And we're happy to work with you for a round.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Some follow-ups?

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Cheers.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: So 1,500, 1,300, these numbers are nowhere near 12,000.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: So what is, how are you all thinking about how you reach 12,000 New Yorkers?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Yeah, so.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: The timeline in the plan itself was to 2040.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Obviously, there's also been the impacts, as we've been noting, of the federal government.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: However, we're laying the groundwork for a lot of that work as well for really increasing equitable opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And I'd say through the negotiations.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: of our project labor agreements, which cover tens of billions of dollars of construction work over the next many, many years across the city, making sure that there are pathways there for New Yorkers to get connected to jobs in the construction sector.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So through community hiring and setting goals on those PLAs, that's a really strong opportunity to maximize connecting New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I would be very interested to see a better breakdown of

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: how that 2040 goal of 12,000 placements will be reached.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I think 1,300 number is very low, and I don't know if that happened

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: If that's a 2026 number or if that's a since 2023 number, would be great to know how you're assessing and evaluating the placements.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I mean, I think having a number deems us to ask, you know, how you got there and how are you going to get there?

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I'll move on.

chapters.summary: Hanif asks for updated figures on the NYCHA Clean Energy Academy, noting 39 residents had completed the 16-week program as of December 2023. Perry does not have the updated numbers and offers to follow up.
chapters.title: Hanif asks about NYCHA Clean Energy Academy updated figures

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: It also mentions NYCHA Clean Energy Academy as a tool for creating economic mobility for low-income New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: As of 2023, December 2023, 39 residents had completed the 16-week program.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: program.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Does NYC Talent or EDC have an updated figure on the number of participants in this program?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Sorry, could you just clarify which program specifically?

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: The NITRA Clean Energy Academy.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I don't think I have those numbers.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Maybe you'd see this, but we'd be happy to work with our colleagues back and get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Would deeply appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you, chairs.

chapters.summary: Won returns and asks if the team has updates on the $600 million breakdown. Perry says they looked into it but need to work with partner agencies, as the $600 million reflects FY24 funding across multiple youth programs like DYCD's Advance and Earn, not exclusively green economy. Won presses on whether the $600 million is specifically for green career pathways as the plan stated.
chapters.title: Won asks for follow-up on $600M green career pathways breakdown

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: I want to go back to what we were talking about earlier.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Does your team have updates on the numbers that we asked for follow-up?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So we did look into it.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Unfortunately, we will have to work closer with our colleagues across the various agencies.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: As I mentioned, that $600 million is a reflection of the FY24 budget, which goes into a variety of programs that serve a broad scope of young people for a broad scope of jobs.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So take, for example, like DYC's Advance and Earn and Learn and Earn.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Those serve out-of-school, out-of-work youth.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: There are specific parts of those programs and pathways for jobs in the green economy, but it's not exclusively for that.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So we'll have to go back and work with our partners at those city agencies to get you a more clarified number.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: But it's supposed to be $600 million in green career pathways, correct?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So it has to be for green career pathways because that's what your plan said.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: The $600 million was specifically just tied to the youth action.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Plan was not exclusively tied to green pathways.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so some of those jobs will, we are happy to work with our partners to try to figure out what that portion of 600 million is specific to green economy, but not all of the 600 million was tied specific to the green economy.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Okay, so when do you think you'll have that follow up for us by?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We will work as soon as we can.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Well, we've worked with our colleagues on getting some of the data.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We didn't get that particular piece.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We're happy to go back and work with them and try to get that as soon as possible.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So when is as soon as possible?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Is that like end of this month?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Is it next month?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I'm happy to work.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I don't want to speak on behalf of some of the complexity in terms of it's a big number and there's a lot of different programs that go into it.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So I'm happy to work and get you an answer as soon as we can.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: $600 million is a very significant amount of taxpayer dollars.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So I think the public deserves to know.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So I hope by end of next month, you will get us those numbers of exactly where the $600 million are being spent for.

chapters.summary: Won asks how the Mamdani administration's $4.5 million GROW pilot from DEP overlaps with the $600 million in existing green career pathways. Perry clarifies the $4.5 million is new, separate funding to train 20 participants in managing rain gardens in East New York and South Ozone Park, not part of the $600 million Youth Action Plan.
chapters.title: Won asks about $4.5M GROW pilot overlap with existing programs

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Can you also help us understand the difference between the $4.5 million in green workforce program pilot?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: How does that...

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Overlap with the 600 million for the existing green career pathways for young people versus the 4.5 million green workforce program pilot by the Mamdani administration.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So just to clarify, you're referring to the GROW program from DEP, correct?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Yeah, so I'd say that's a reflection of this administration's commitment to following through on what we've outlined already.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: The Green Economy Action Plan.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so that investment from DEP is a reflection of the belief of that climate change is real.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We need to continue to invest in the Green Economy Action Plan.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So that investment is not...

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: At the time of the Green Economy Action Plan was not explicitly outlined, but as we know, the plan was based on projections, based on information at the time, but this administration continues to adapt and,

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: as seen here, invest in good opportunities to connect

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: people working with the DOH fund.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So am I understanding you correctly?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: You're saying that the $4.5 million is not net new, it's for the existing program?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: No, this was a new $4.5 million pilot administered by DEP.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So that was a new investment by DEP.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Okay, and what is different?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: You're saying that it's a completely different set of funding and it's being used for different purposes?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: It's being used to help train 20 participants in managing over a thousand different rain gardens in East New York and South Ozone Park.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Okay, so it's not overlapping with the 600 million.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: This is a completely separate program from what the Adams administration already had?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So again, just to clarify, the $600 million is not part of the $600 million because it's not part of the funding that was outlined in the Youth Action Plan.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so this is a separate $4.5 million investment that was made by DEP just recently.

chapters.summary: Won asks whether the $600 million will continue to be funded in the next fiscal year starting July 1. Perry says it was a point-in-time reflection of FY24 and he cannot guarantee all programs will continue at the same levels. Won presses on whether the overall GEEP will be funded, and Perry says the budget is not yet finalized but expects stable funding for Talent's green economy portions.
chapters.title: Won asks if $600M and GEEP will continue to be funded next fiscal year

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Okay, so this isn't a new relabeling under the $600 million youth action plan.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: This is not part of the youth action plan money.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: This is additional funding in addition to that $600 million part of the youth action plan.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: But the $600 million is going to continue to get funded under this administration in the upcoming budget?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So the $600 million was a point in time reflection of what was then fiscal year 2024.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Again, we'd have to work closer with our partners at OMB and...

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So you're not sure if this is going to continue to get funded?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Many of these programs are continuing to get funded.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: For example, SYEP was counted as part of that, and so SYEP is a robust program.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: There's many other programs that go into it.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I don't have all of that data in front of me.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: The programs are part of the $600 million.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I would have to get back to you.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: How many agencies?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: It includes New York City Public Schools, CUNY, DYCD, and maybe some others as well.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so I have some samples of it, but I don't have a full accounting of every single program.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: It was a full roundup of...

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: One year's youth funding.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Are you not concerned that it may or may not be fully funded by next fiscal year, which starts end of this month?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I just want to clarify, it's not a question of whether it, for me, it's not a question of whether it will or will not be.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I just don't know.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Most specifically each line item program and what program may continue and has continued.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I do know though that SYEP is continuing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So you can guarantee the public that all $600 million will continue to be funded by next fiscal year starting July 1st?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I can't make that commitment.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I would need to talk to my colleagues at

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: across all of these agencies to figure out, again, this was a few, three fiscal years ago, and so where these programs are, maybe some of them have wrapped up, maybe some of them are more enhanced.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I don't have the specific number that would sort of be an analog to that $600 million point in time.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So the agencies have been keeping up with this program for the last few fiscal years?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: The agencies, our partner agencies on the youth side, we're incredibly proud of the work that they do and the work that we do to partner with them, and they do a lot of work.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: But because I don't have this data specifically on all of the youth action plan, because again, the youth action plan is an important,

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: plan, however, it was not tied exclusively to the Green Economy Action Plan, so I don't have all of the data tied to all of the youth work that the city has done, but the city does quite a bit.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So is the overall Green Economy Action Plan going to be funded in the next fiscal year?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So I know the budget is still not yet finalized, and so I don't have all the details, and we're happy to work with our partners on what the green economy investments may look like and how we at Talent can help support them.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We're expecting on the Talent side stable funding related to our green economy portions, but we'll have to work with our colleagues.

chapters.summary: Won asks about Talent's portion of green economy funding. Perry says since 2024 they've driven $13 million to green economy work. Won asks how much is city funding and whether the green economy is a priority of the mayor. Perry says the administration is committed to environmental and economic justice under Deputy Mayor Julie Hsu. Won asks for the total FY27 funding amount and Perry says they need to work with OMB.
chapters.title: Won asks about Talent's green funding portion and whether GEEP is mayor's priority

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So what's the portion for the Talent side?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: For the Green Economy Action Plan?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So since 2024, we've driven more than $13 million to work in the green economy, but most, and that's like both staff, OTPS costs, and private funding, some federal funding, some capital funding, some CTL.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So how much of the $13 million is city funding?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I don't have the exact number on that 13 million, but again, most of the work that talent does is actually supporting other agencies and their investments.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so once the FY27 budget is finalized and announced, we work closely with all of our partners across the city to see how we can support those investments.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And when there are green economy investments, of course, we will- Is this a priority of the mayor, the green economy action plan?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Like I said in our testimony, this administration is deeply committed to environmental justice.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We're excited about the leadership of Deputy Mayor Julie Hsu and being the first ever deputy mayor for economic justice, and I think economic justice and environmental justice.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So if it's a priority, what is the total amount of funding that's going to be promised?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: this year that's going to be voted on next week on Tuesday, the latest, for the Green Economy Action Plan?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So the city's investment across all sorts of things, and the green economy is quite large, so we would have to

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: work with OMB and our numerous agencies to get a detailed accounting of all of the investments that are tied to the green economy for FY27.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Is this a priority of the mayor?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Like I said, we are absolutely committed to the green economy and to making sure that we are growing it equitably and making sure that we are

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: supporting New Yorkers and connecting to the jobs, despite the loss of the federal funding that has come through.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Both EDC and I have testified today, there is still robust work happening to make sure that we have a good foundation to support all of the jobs that are still around.

chapters.summary: Won expresses surprise and frustration that despite the hearing being planned for weeks and the mayor's claimed priority, every question about specific numbers has gone unanswered. She notes the plan commits to training facilities in every borough and asks how many are open today.
chapters.title: Won expresses frustration at lack of numbers despite claimed priority

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: I'm just surprised because we've had this in plan for weeks and the council has been very upfront about this hearing, but every single question that I've asked, you don't have any of the numbers for, yet you tell me that this is a

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: priority of the mayor, but we don't know any of the data that we need to make sure that the public understands this truly is a priority of the mayor.

chapters.summary: Won asks about training facility status and capital funding. Jung confirms only Solar One in Manhattan is currently open, with Batworks (Brooklyn, late 2028), Bronx Green Job Center (end of year), and CUNY campuses in Queens and Staten Island (timelines TBD). Won asks about capital funding allocations; Jung says investments were opportunistic rather than strategically budgeted, with Batworks receiving $100 million, Bronx $3 million, and CUNY $10 million.
chapters.title: Won asks about training facilities open, timelines, and capital funding

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So the plan commits to developing a green collar workforce training facility in every single borough.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: How many of these facilities are open today and what is the timeline for the rest that are unopened?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I'll pass it to my colleagues at EDC.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: In Brooklyn at Batworks, at the Brooklyn Army Terminal, there's going to be the largest workforce development training amounting to 30,000 square feet.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: That's going to be opening late 2028.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: In the Bronx, we've invested $3 million into Fordham's Bronx Green Job Center, which is going to be 12,000 square feet.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: That is on track to open by the end of the calendar year.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: In Manhattan, the Solar One Environmental Education Center in St.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Vincent Cove, Midtown East, 6,500 square feet, is already open.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Which one is open?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The Solar One Environmental Education Center.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: What borough?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We've made $10 million investments into various CUNY campuses.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: The one in Queens will be at Queensborough Community College.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: They're developing an energy education center in Staten Island at the College of Staten Island.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So what year will the Queens one be open?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: So both for the Queens and College of Staten Island.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Seton Island Center, which will be focused on sustainable business, finance, and entrepreneurship.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We're actively liaising with the community central capital team to get a firmer answer on when they will be able to develop those centers at those campuses.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I'm happy to get back to you on those timelines.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Okay, so currently there's only one open.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: In Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And this has all been done within the past two years.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And so given the nature of construction development life cycles, we're proud to say that that's been open.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: And how much capital funding has been allocated to all five boroughs and for each site?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We haven't had a strategic budget allocation.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We've opportunistically have invested in these different workforce facilities as opportunities come up with our eye on developing a center in each borough.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I will say that the Batworks overall project is going to receive $100 million of investment.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Like I mentioned before, the Bronx Fordham's Bronx Green Job Center has received $3 million from us.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We supported Solar One Environmental Education Center with their development.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I don't have the exact figure on hand.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I'm happy to share that figure at a later time.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And like I mentioned before, the $10 million of investment went into CUNY-wide, and we're happy to get you those discrete figures for which are associated with those campuses in the future.

chapters.summary: Won asks how many people have been trained at Solar One (Jung doesn't have the number but says CUNY campuses will train 6,000 annually once open). Won notes only one facility is open, the 12,000 apprenticeship target is far from being met, and the Center for Urban Future found only 2,184 net new green jobs in 2023. She asks if EDC accepts this figure.
chapters.title: Won asks about Solar One training numbers, 12K target gap, and CUF figure

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: How many people have been trained at Solar One?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I don't have that discrete number, but I'm happy to get back to you at a later point in time.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: For the community campuses, once all

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: of those are opened, they're expected to train 6,000 students annually.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Okay, because the plan was setting a target of more than 12,000 New Yorkers for green occupations through the apprenticeship and pre-apprenticeship programs, but it seems that only one is currently open and the number of people trained is unknown.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: And the Center for Urban Future, as you heard from the chair, only added roughly 2,184 net new green jobs in 2023.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Do you accept this figure?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: And if not, what is your current count, which is what we asked you earlier, but you did not have an answer for us for?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We haven't seen that figure.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: It doesn't seem to align with the methodology that we've deployed.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: So we're happy to come back to you once we consult with our data team that has not been able to produce a number at this point in time.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: After two years, you don't have any figure of how many people you've trained for net new green jobs?

chapters.summary: Jung says EDC hasn't seen the CUF figure and it doesn't align with their methodology. He says EDC has trained about 300 people across its programs. Perry provides additional numbers: approximately 2,300 adult workers and 750 young adults served through green economy programs, including 1,500 through PINC, 150+ through Cool Roofs, 375+ through pre-apprenticeship, 300 city employees through DCAS EV training, and 350+ students through Inside Industrial.
chapters.title: Jung and Perry provide actual training and placement numbers

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: It depends on different programs specifically.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: For the ones that we have been leading, we've trained about 300 overall across various programs that we've invested in.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: But I'll let my colleague, Doug, if you have any other.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Data on your programs.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
