utterances.text: And so, like I mentioned in the testimony, we estimate about, since the Green Economy Action Plan was released, about 2,300 adult workers and 750 young adults have been served through the Green Economy.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So that's almost 1,500 through that PINK program that I mentioned as being connected to union and prevailing wage careers.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: SBS through their Cool Roofs program, which I mentioned has the NYC Talent Help Secure funding for.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: They've connected.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Over 150 people, as well as their investment in their pre-apprenticeship program, over 375 people.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We've also trained, as I mentioned in the testimony, or rather DCAS has trained through funding we helped secure for them, 300 city employees to learn safety around electric vehicles.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And then

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: On the young people side, we have our Inside Industrial program, which, Chair Wana, we were grateful for you to come visit as we had a tour at IBEW Local 3 a few months back.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: That's a career exposure program for New York City public school students, and that's a program that we've trained over about 350 young people.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We also have our Youth Apprentice Civil Service title, which NYC Talent worked closely with our partners, DCAS, to secure a brand new civil service title, which was a really exciting opportunity for young people.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so we have 17 apprentices at city agencies that are working in the green economy, like DEP, DOT, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so we estimate, like I said, today roughly 2,300 adults, 750 young people that have already been connected.

chapters.summary: Won asks if the city is on pace toward the 12,000 apprenticeship target and about current energy storage capacity. Perry says approximately 900 of the 1,500 PINC participants are apprentices, with 17 youth apprentices at city agencies and 12 more starting this fall. He emphasizes community hiring and PLAs as future pathways to scale apprenticeships.
chapters.title: Won asks about pace toward 12K target and energy storage capacity

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Are we on pace towards a 12,000 apprenticeship target?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: And what is our current count against the 200 MW or 500 million baseline reported in the plan?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: And what is the current induced storage capacity?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Oh, 200 megawatts.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So of that pink number that I mentioned, about 1,500, approximately 900 of them are apprentices that were connected through pink.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And like I said, we had 17 young people working at agencies in the green economy at sanitation, DOT, parks, DEP, DDC.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And we have another 12 young people starting apprenticeships this upcoming school year, and we're looking to continue that.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And as I mentioned as well, we have our community hiring initiative, which is just coming online with contracts just beginning, and there will be apprenticeship goals on those contracts.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So as we look towards that 2040 horizon, we're really excited about the opportunity of community hiring and our project labor agreements to

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: increase the opportunities for low-income New Yorkers, including our neighbors in NYCHA, to be connected to apprenticeships in New York.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: The construction trades.

chapters.summary: Won asks EDC and NYC Talent to walk through each of the seven marquee initiatives from the plan with original targets, current status, timelines, and revised metrics. Perry provides a detailed response on community hiring, noting 280+ procurements valued at $14 billion, but does not have specific targets for each initiative.
chapters.title: Won asks for status of seven marquee initiatives with targets and timelines

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: My last question is the Green Economy Action Plan named seven marquee initiatives, one per strategic goal, so for community hiring and place-based workforce strategies, tax incentives for battery storage, public sites for EV charging, Harbor Climate Collaborative,

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Climate Innovation Hub at Brooklyn,

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Army terminal, green training facilities in every borough, and implicitly getting local law 97 done.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So I think I already know the answer to this, but for the record, can you walk through each of these seven marquee initiatives identified in the plan and provide A, an original target and metric in which you put out these forecasts and these goals, B, current status against that target,

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: C, timeline to completion, and D, any metric that has been revised since publication, and Y.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So I want to make sure I'm responding to all of the initiatives as best we can.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I would say with community hiring, like I said, back in 2024 when this plan was released, we were still in the rulemaking process of community hiring.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so these rules went into effect in January 2025.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And at that point, we worked across all of our city agency partners to start setting goals on those.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Community hiring contracts.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so we're proud that over 280 procurements have been released valued at approximately $14 billion.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And these are applicable to all sorts of contracts, but including green economy related investments, such as construction of bioswales, resiliency infrastructure, building services, consulting services like engineering and design.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So really opening up opportunities in sectors that traditionally have not been common sectors served by the workforce system.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so we're excited about community hiring being able to do that.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I don't, there were not specific goals tied to community hiring numbers in the Green Economy Action Plan, but like was mentioned, the plan was a projection based on information at the time.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We are excited still today of making sure that we are continuing to connect New Yorkers to jobs in the green economy and want to make sure that all New Yorkers have access and opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So a lot of the work that we've done, whether it's through our remake team who is conducting webinars and learning from both employers, big and small,

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: our work of putting community hiring out there, and then a lot of the programming that we've already touched on today, making sure that we're setting New Yorkers up.

chapters.summary: Won asks EDC and NYC Talent to commit to outlining total funding and updated job projections annually. Perry says they're happy to share data but notes the 400,000 was a projection, not a goal, and the methodology is complex. He mentions a legislatively mandated climate plan coming next year. Won presses for a commitment to annual updates, and Perry says they need to work with colleagues. Won reiterates that only 2,184 net new green jobs have been added since 2023, calling it abysmal for $600 million spent.
chapters.title: Won asks for annual funding and job projection commitment; reiterates 2,184 net new jobs

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: There seems to be a lot of lack of clarity in today's hearing.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Can EDC and New York City Talent commit to outlining total funding committed towards the goals of the Green Economy Action Plan and updated projections of the green economy jobs by 2040 every single year?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: And if every year is not achievable, what do you think is a reasonable timeline for the public?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: To have accountability and transparency?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So we're happy to try to share as much data as we can.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Like I mentioned, at the time the report was published, it was projections, right?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So it was not a goal necessarily.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: It was based on information, a lot of which unfortunately is not there because of the federal investment that was lost.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so,

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We're excited to make sure that we are maximizing the investments that we do have and that are continuing to connect New Yorkers to jobs.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We're also excited about the leadership of the Louis Young, the chief climate officer, and there will be a climate plan coming out as legislatively mandated next year.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so this administration is definitely committed to continuing to really make sure that there is economic justice, environmental justice, and that from talent we are really making sure that we are

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: making all of these investments available to New Yorkers where there are job opportunities there.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So is that a no, you aren't going to commit to a timeline to get us new projections?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Because if it's a projection and there have been changes on the policy level on every single level of government, there should be a new projection, especially if it's a priority of the Mamdani administration.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So I know that there was a specific, as my colleague mentioned, the specific methodology that was used in terms of, I'm not sure of the level of effort associated with updating that on a regular basis.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I think the most important part for us is making sure that we are maximizing all investments to connect New Yorkers to good jobs that we have.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so we're happy to.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: work with you, Chair, on getting you more data to help, getting you and the public more data to help make clear any questions that you have.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And, you know, this administration is absolutely committed to following through on connecting New Yorkers to jobs in the green economy.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So will you commit to giving us an annual update?

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Because that's what the question was.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We'd have to work back with our colleagues at Talent and at EDC.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: A lot of this data that we are using and all of the commitments are spread broadly across the city.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So we're happy to work with our colleagues and our partner agencies with OMB and City Hall to try to get you as much data as we possibly can.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Since 2023, I want to reiterate that the only number we have is 2,184 net new green jobs.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: So that's pretty abysmal for $600 million being spent.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks about updated NYCHA Clean Energy Academy participant numbers (last reported 39 in December 2023), internship and job placement data from training programs, and green economy job vacancies in city agencies. Perry does not have the specific numbers but offers to follow up, noting over 100 active youth apprentices at city agencies with 17 in green economy roles.
chapters.title: Maloney asks about NYCHA Clean Energy Academy, job placements, and city agency vacancies

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I had follow-up questions on the talent pipeline and economic justice and inclusion initiatives.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Specifically, the NYCHA Clean Energy Academy was mentioned as a key tool for creating economic mobility and green economy jobs for low-income New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And our data is from December 2023, saying 39 residents had gone through the program.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I'm wondering if you have an updated figure on the number of participants from the program.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: As I mentioned earlier, we don't have the updated figure, but we're happy to go back and work with our partners to try to get you that.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And the placements in terms of number of people that were placed in internships or full-time roles within city agencies, do you have any of that data as well?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Specifically with internships at city agencies, we have...

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I don't have all of that data in front of me, but as I mentioned, our youth apprentice title, which is connecting New York City public school students to apprenticeships, paid on-the-job training at various city agencies.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I think we have, I think right now, over 100 active apprentices at city agencies.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We have even more that have come through the program.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And right now we have 17 who are at agencies that do work in the green economy, so like DEP, DOT.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We have another 12 joining for this upcoming fall cohort.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We'd have to get back to you on sort of all of the, there's a whole suite of programs of internships and apprenticeships and fellowships that.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Happen to connect, that work to connect young New Yorkers to job opportunities with the city.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so we don't have that full number, but happy to work and try to get that to you.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And with the training programs, not just completion rates of the program, but then job placements that came afterwards.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Is that something you can follow up with as well?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We'll follow up with you as well on that.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And do you have a sense of how many green economy jobs are available?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: in our own city agencies and what the vacancy rate is with those positions.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I don't have that data in front of me, but we are definitely interested in maximizing opportunities for city agencies.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I've said this before and always make a plug, I love working for the city, and so trying to get more young people into the city is really important to us.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so we're happy to work with our partners at DCAS and at those agencies that do work in the green economy, which spans many, many agencies, as we know.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: To get you that data.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks about AI and tech industry threats to green economy jobs given energy demands expected to double by 2030. Perry notes MOCEJ is coordinating with state entities on data center loads. Jung says NYC's data centers are not at hyperscaler scale, and that 70% of green economy jobs are transitioning trades involving physical work that can't be displaced by AI.
chapters.title: Maloney asks about AI impact on green economy and energy demands

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: One other question is around the rise of AI and tech industries that may or may not pose a threat to green economy jobs.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: The demands on our energy needs are expected to double by 2030, and I'm wondering if you could give us an update on any concerns around the action plan or shifts in the action plan

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: related to the increase in AI.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Yeah, we share your concern, Chair Maloney, on the environmental impacts of data centers.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: MOCEJ is in active coordination with various state entities, including the Public Service Commission, to submit comments around large loads, which incorporates loads from data centers, among other infrastructure projects.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so we take our direction from a policy perspective from that team.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: In terms of data centers within the city, there isn't given real...

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: estate constraints, data centers at the scale at which hyperscalers are trying to develop.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: There are some that support different financial institutions and edge computing at various sites across the city, but they're not at the scale

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: that would create significant environmental and energy burdens or job displacement impact for local New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We are keeping an active pulse on what AI means for jobs more broadly, including those potentially within the green economy, but given that 70%

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: of the green economy jobs that we forecast will be realized are transitioning jobs, those are accountants, those in the trades, the nature of green economy work often involves physical work.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Remaking our infrastructure, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: So we're quite confident that that can't be replaced or displaced wholesale by AI, but we're happy to keep you apprised of any analysis and different insights that we get sharper on in the future as that becomes more of a clarifying focus for us.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks about the strength of the city's electric grid amid increasing demands. Jung cites a NYISO reliability report showing narrowing reliability margins and shares concerns about securing reliable, low-cost energy. He notes EDA has supported 400+ megawatts of clean technology deployment and sees battery storage as viable for addressing both reliability and extreme heat risks.
chapters.title: Maloney asks about electric grid strength amid increased demands

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: How do you think of the strength of the city's infrastructure and the electric grid in the face of these increased demands?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, the NYSO put out a reliability report a little bit ago.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: It outlined that the reliability margins are narrowing, and so we do see a potential challenge with

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: securing reliable and low-cost energy for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: So I do share your concern about

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: that potential becoming a larger challenge.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I think for us, we are doing all that we can to support different clean energy technologies that will alleviate burden, make the grid more resilient, and make energy more affordable.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: So we're happy to share that IDA, in particular, has supported more than 400 megawatts of clean energy

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: clean technology deployment across the city.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Those have actively mitigated peak demands, particularly during the summer months.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: As we all know, there's 500 New Yorkers that die every summer from extreme heat.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And so we see batteries as a viable technology to address not only the reliability

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: needs that we see coming down the pike, but also to solve immediate concerns, particularly around extreme heat in the here and now to not only save New Yorkers'lives, but also create economic opportunities for our green economy industries today.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks how EDC coordinates with city agencies on grid modernization. Jung says EDC participates in MOCEJ-led interagency working groups, bringing infrastructure development perspectives to conversations about large loads and policy objectives.
chapters.title: Maloney asks about grid modernization coordination with city agencies

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And the initiatives around modernization of that electric grid as there continues to be more pressure on it.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Can you speak to how you're coordinating with city agencies on that work, especially as this problem gets increasingly urgent?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Yeah, thanks for that question, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We're an active participant in MOC EJ-led interagency working groups that are tackling this challenge.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And so we come to the table with a key and I towards how our larger infrastructure development projects, including the large loads that they will require, are an active conversation with other policy objectives.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: That MOCJ is trying to solve.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: But we are equally concerned about the issues that you shared and are coming to the table as problem solvers.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Chair, do you have any other final questions?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks whether a permanent EDC president has been appointed and about the timeline. Jung says there is no permanent president, but interim president Jeannie Pak has kept EDC laser-focused on delivery, citing Batworks temporary space opening, solar awards, and a microgrid RFI. He does not know the timeline or interview status for a permanent appointment.
chapters.title: Maloney asks about permanent EDC president status and timeline

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: My last set of questions is for EDC.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: We are now nearing July.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Do you have a permanent president of the New York City Economic Development Corporation?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We currently don't have a permanent president, as you know, but I will say, and as you are intimately familiar with, the talent at EDC are some of the brightest minds really driven to make public impact.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Our interim president, Jeannie Pak, has harnessed that commitment to ensure that we are, as an organization, laser focused on delivery, including delivering on initiatives within the Green Economy Action Plan.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: For example, we just opened the Batworks temporary space at the Brooklyn Army Terminal in May, which has hosted more than 20 events so far.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We've also awarded about 2.6 megawatts of rooftop and carport solar to developers at sites that we manage in the Bronx and in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: We also issued a microgrid RFI to solicit from industry what a viable microgrid might look like at the Brooklyn Army Terminal to advance climate innovation, technology, piloting, and testing.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And I will note that interim president Jeannie Pak has been

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Closely collaborating with Deputy Mayor Julie Hsu on broader administrative priorities, which includes issuing the modular bathrooms,

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: making sure that we as EDC are leading the charge on public groceries, and we're moving full steam ahead and the work has not slowed.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Do you have a sense of the timeline of when a new president will be put in place?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I unfortunately do not.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Do you have a sense of how many people have been interviewed or where they are in that process?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I unfortunately am not privy to that information.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks about morale at EDC without a permanent president. Jung says EDC staff are motivated by their mission of creating economic opportunities. Perry adds that staff love interim President Pak, an EDC veteran who knows the work, and that the organization has not felt a lack of direction. Maloney asks if the interim title hinders long-term vision; Perry says no, citing public grocery and insurance captive projects. Maloney concludes by stressing the importance of installing a permanent president.
chapters.title: Maloney asks about morale and long-term vision; concludes with call for permanent president

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: How is the lack of a permanent president impacting day-to-day morale of employees at NYC EDC?

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: That's a good question.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Thanks, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I think generally, obviously, people are curious about what will happen.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: But like I mentioned before, EDCers are at EDC to drive impact, to create economic opportunities for everyday New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: I think we as staff feel lucky that that is our nine to five that we can do every single day.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And so we're motivated by the higher purpose of supporting and serving New Yorkers and realizing economic opportunities for everybody.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: And so I think that's our laser focus now and implementing on key commitments that we have, including in the Jeep, but otherwise.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Sorry, the only thing that I would add, Shermaloney, is at EDC, we love Interim President Pack.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We think that she is doing an amazing job.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so we have not really felt.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: a lack of any direction.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: As my colleague said, we're in constant contact with DM Sue, her team in the administration, and President Pack has really driven us forward.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: She is an EDC-er, she knows our work, so in some ways, right, it's been a great transition for us in that we have someone who's fully on board and ready to move forward.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And we're just excited, I think, about the new things that the admin's giving us to do.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We have never been busier, which is a good thing, but no additional information, but we're really happy with President Pack.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I agree.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: The interim president has done a fantastic job of leading the organization.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Do you believe there's been any hindrance on the ability to have a long-term vision or long-term plan when the title is interim president?

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I don't think so.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: I mean, we have really been like moving ahead full steam.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: We're moving full steam ahead, at least from the work that I've seen.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: Like we haven't stopped.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: It hasn't prohibited us from working closely with the administration as we roll out big new projects.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: So I think things like public grocery, things like the insurance captive, like did really require a lot of long term vision.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And we've been able to execute on that with President Pack, interim President Pack.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And so from an operational perspective, no, we still continue to like ideate like broadly into the future.

utterances.speaker: Douglas Perry
utterances.text: And work closely with the admin to do that.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony and for anyone listening.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I think it's very important and crucial at this juncture to install a permanent president with the full authority to set the direction.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: for our economic development plan.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Maloney transitions to public testimony, reminding attendees of decorum rules, two-minute time limit per speaker, and procedures for submitting written testimony within 72 hours.
chapters.title: Maloney transitions to public testimony with instructions

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I now conclude that part of the testimony and want to open up to public testimony.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Julie Won
utterances.text: Thank you, chairs.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: I want to remind members of the public that this is a formal government proceeding and that decorum should be observed at all times.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So please remain silent during testimony.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: The witness table is reserved for people who wish to testify.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: No video recording or photography is allowed from the witness table and members of the public may not present audio or video recordings.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: If you wish to speak today, please fill out an appearance card with the Sergeant at Arms and wait to be recognized, and we will have two minutes per speaker on the Green Economy Action Plan topic.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: If you have a written statement,

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Please provide a copy of that to the Sergeant in his arms as well.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And you may email written testimony to testimony at council.myc.gov within 72 hours of this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Audio or video recordings will not be accepted.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: First up, Gregory Morris, Angela Sun,

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Carrie Fallhaber, and Christopher Maloney.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Same last name.

utterances.speaker: Sam Jung
utterances.text: Can start whenever you're ready.

chapters.summary: Greg Morris, CEO of the NYC Employment and Training Coalition, introduces his organization's network of 220 workforce providers serving hundreds of thousands of New Yorkers. He notes the difficulty of getting budget and outcome answers in workforce development, stresses the need for a permanent EDC president, and introduces the workforce providers who will testify.
chapters.title: Greg Morris (NYC Employment and Training Coalition) on workforce providers and EDC president

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: Good afternoon, chairs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: My name is Greg Morris.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: I'm the CEO of the New York City Employment and Training Coalition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: I do have some prepared remarks and recommendations as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: I'll put those to the side and offer those to the chairs separately.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: What I wanted to say at this point is my responsibility of the New York City Employment and Training Coalition

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: is to lead the coalition of providers and entities in New York City that are focused on workforce development.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: And the green space is critically important to these providers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: 220 folks within, 220 organizations within our network, serving as many as a couple hundred thousand New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: And many of those providers have a hand in how it is that the city should be, could be,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: and is thinking about the green economy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: I am very proud today to have invited a bunch of them to come to this space, and they're going to speak during the public session and tell their story as to what innovation looks like and what impact looks like and how it is that our

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: city can transform in all the right ways to be able to find success in facilitating access to green jobs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: And I look forward to them sharing their stories, and those are critical.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: I just want to say a few things in relation to what we just heard.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: I suspect if you did a hearing on health care initiatives related to jobs in New York City or the tech space career pathways,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: a lot of what you just put forward in terms of your questions about how much is something costing and are we on track to meet the expectations that we set and how is it that communities are benefiting?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: I suspect you'd have a difficult time getting answers to those two.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: We in the workforce development space understand that our work is challenging and meets the needs of New Yorkers with the variety of challenges and vulnerabilities and pathways to success.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: The complexity of not being able to say what our city budget is to solve that problem is tremendously impactful and puts us at a disadvantage to be able to ensure that we are making sure that folks work towards careers and opportunities that lead to good jobs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: Last but not least, I'll just reinforce the message that I've offered and will continue to offer.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: And Chair Maloney, thank you for putting this forward.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: We need to have a president of EDC in place to ensure that economic development and economic mobility is moving forward in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: And again, Councilmember Wan, thank you very much for championing the Workforce Development Committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: It's the first of its kind.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: I know that has lots of opportunities and challenges associated with it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Greg Morris
utterances.text: This is the group that's ready to work with you to make that possible.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Council member doesn't have the same ring to it, nor does interim president.

chapters.summary: Carrie Fallhaber of Jobs First NYC describes the Green Economy Network of 200+ organizations that has trained 7,000+ New Yorkers for green jobs since 2023. She offers three recommendations: establish a workforce authority within City Hall, strengthen coordination between schools and industry, and make industry a full partner in implementing the GEEP.
chapters.title: Carrie Fallhaber (Jobs First NYC) on Green Economy Network and recommendations

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: Good afternoon, chairs and members of committees on workforce development and economic development.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify today on the implementation of the Green Economy Action Plan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: My name is Carrie Fallhaber.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: I'm the Senior Vice President of Jobs First NYC.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: Jobs First NYC creates and advances solutions that strengthen education and workforce systems and expand economic opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: We work alongside hundreds of partners across all five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: Through our Green Economy Network, more than 200 nonprofit workforce organizations, employers, city agencies, and educational institutions and intermediaries have collaborated since 2019 to prepare New Yorkers for careers in the green economy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: Since 2023 alone, the network has trained more than 7,000 New Yorkers for green jobs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: The Green Economy Action Plan presents an incredible opportunity with a projected 400,000 green jobs by 2040, but those jobs will not exist simply because they are projected.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: They will exist only if the city treats workforce development as core infrastructure by aligning public investment, education, workforce strategy, and industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: To realize this vision, we offer three recommendations on top of recommendations that we put out earlier this year.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: First, establish a workforce authority within City Hall with the authority to align agencies, coordinate workforce strategy, and embed workforce outcomes into climate procurement and capital investments.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: Second, strengthen coordination between schools, workforce providers, and industry so education, training, and work-based learning are aligned with the skills needed to meet current and emerging labor market demand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: Third, make industry a full partner in implementing the Green Economy Action Plan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: Employers create demand, define emerging skill needs, and determine whether the city's workforce investments translate into jobs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: Industry partners can validate emerging occupations, anticipate hiring needs, and keep workforce investments aligned with real market demand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: The Green Economy Action Plan isn't economic strategy as much as it is a climate strategy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: Success will be measured not by plans written or programs launched, but by whether New Yorkers are hired into quality careers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: With stronger coordination, clear accountability, and sustained industry partnership, New York City can build the workforce needed to power a greener economy while expanding access to family-sustaining careers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Carrie Fallhaber
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Angela Nuri-San, founder of Green Launchpad, co-leads an employer-led workforce coalition representing 2,400+ clean energy professionals. She urges treating workforce as a business competitiveness issue rather than a social obligation, noting most green economy companies are small contractors without HR departments. She calls for sustained employer-provider partnerships and measuring success by retention, not just enrollment.
chapters.title: Angela Nuri-San (Green Launchpad) on employer-driven workforce solutions

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: Good afternoon, thank you for the opportunity to testify.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: My name is Angela Nuri-San, I'm founder and CEO of the Green Launchpad, and we co-lead NYC

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: Energy Efficiency Employer-Led Workforce Coalition, long name, with NYC ETC,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: bringing together employers who represent over 2,400 clean energy professionals, and together we design practical employer-driven workforce solutions.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: So as the city advances the Green Economy Action Plan, I encourage you to treat workforce not just as a pipeline issue, but actually as a business competitiveness issue.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: The challenge isn't that employers don't want to hire.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: It's really that we too frequently ask them to engage in workforce development as a social obligation, maybe a box to check, rather than offering it as a solution to a problem that they already have.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: We have to remember that companies engage when it matters and when it makes their businesses stronger.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: And it does.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: Most of our green economy actually runs

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: on small and mid-sized contractors with no HR departments and no recruiters.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: For them, a coordinated workforce provider isn't a charity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: It's an extension of the business that finds qualified candidates, prepares them for actual work, and supports retention after hire.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: That actually saves them time, reduces turnover, and lets them focus on growing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: So as the plan should treat workforce providers as part of the city's business support infrastructure alongside financing and procurement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: Specifically, we asked the city to invest in sustained employer-provider partnerships, not just one-off programs, fund intermediaries that translate employer need into training,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: incentivize employers to co-design curricula and work-based learning, and actually measure success by retention and advancement, not just enrollment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: So I think we can strengthen that coordination and workforce development stops being an ask.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: It becomes a competitive advantage for businesses in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Dr. Christopher Malone of the Consortium for Worker Education submits a 10-point green workforce acceleration strategy, highlighting six: scale pre-apprenticeship pipeline, align investments to LL97 implementation, create a public-facing green workforce dashboard, strengthen Workforce One centers, invest in worker supports like childcare and ESOL, and advance a near-term workforce surge strategy.
chapters.title: Christopher Malone (Consortium for Worker Education) on 10-point green workforce strategy

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Wan, Chair Maloney, and the members of the committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: My name is Dr.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: Christopher Malone, and I serve as the Deputy Executive Director of Policy and Education Innovation at the Consortium for Worker Education.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: From CWE's work on the ground, one thing is clear.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: Local Law 97 is already driving urgent workforce demand, and our current pipeline is not yet scaled or aligned to meet it.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: So to meet this moment and in the implementation of the Jeep, we are submitting, for the record, a 10-point green workforce acceleration strategy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: For the sake of time, I just want to focus on six of those items.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: First, scale the pipeline.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: The city must invest not just in apprenticeships, but in tens of thousands of pre-apprenticeship opportunities to meet demand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: Second, align the system to meet real demand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: Workforce investments must be explicitly tied to Local Law 97 implementation, including tracking and placements into priority occupations like electricians, HVAC technicians, and building operators.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: Third, create accountability through data.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: CWE recommends a public-facing green workforce dashboard to track outcomes, placements, apprenticeship, entry, and retention so progress can be measured in real time.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: We do this with our Jobs to Build On program and would be happy to connect to discuss the requisite information the council should mandate the city provide through a reporting bill.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: Number four, strengthen the front door.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: Workforce One centers should implement

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: Clear apprenticeship readiness standards and referral pathways aligned with union entry requirements.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: Fifth, invest in workers'ability to complete training.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: Scaling childcare, ESOL, and other supports is essential to ensuring New Yorkers can actually

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: access these careers, and lastly, advance a near-term workforce surge strategy to rapidly expand training capacity over the next two to three years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: As I said, we are submitting and writing a 10-point plan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: I don't have time to go through all of those, but I and CWE would be happy to work with the council and your committees to put these ideas into actionable policy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: Thanks so much.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you for your testimony.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Next up, we have Jonathan Spooner, Sindri Manazanares, Jake Douglas Boren, and Sophia Oliveira.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Angela Nuri-San
utterances.text: You may begin, yeah.

chapters.summary: Jonathan Spooner of Stacks and Jewels describes how nonprofits operate major real estate portfolios with energy as their second largest expense. He proposes a coordinated funding program for building automation that creates a workforce pipeline while redirecting energy savings back into social services, asking the committee to champion the initiative.
chapters.title: Jonathan Spooner (Stacks and Jewels) on building automation for nonprofits

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: There we go.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: Good afternoon, council members.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to testify.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: I'm Johnson Struner, co-founder of Stacks and Jewels.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: We work at the intersection of building technology and community impact in New York City in partnership with Henry Street Settlement, NYC ETC, and the Green Economy Network.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: Nonprofits across this city quietly operate a major portfolio of real estate.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: Shelters, health clinics, community centers, affordable housing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: They're mission driven, but they're also building operators.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: And that role carries real cost.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: Energy is often a nonprofit's second largest expense after staffing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: For an organization running a domestic violence shelter or mental health clinic, every dollar lost to an inefficient boiler is a dollar not reaching the people walking through the door.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: Add local law 97 penalties and the financial pressure competes directly with their mission.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: But building automation, smart controls, sensors, energy management, can cut energy use by 30%.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: But the upfront cost and complexity put it out of reach for most nonprofits without public support.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: Here's the opportunity for the community.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: A coordinated funding program for building automation does two things at once.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: First, it creates a durable workforce pipeline installing and maintaining these systems as a skilled, well-paying job that can't be offshored, and we can train New Yorkers from the very communities these nonprofits serve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: Second, the energy savings can be redirected back into those social services, meaningfully replacing operating budget and restoring to mission.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: This needs three things, a capital funding mechanism, a workforce training partner, and a champion in this chamber.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: We are asking this committee to be that champion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jonathan Spooner
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

chapters.summary: Cindy Manzanares of Green City Force describes their AmeriCorps program for young adults 16-24 from public housing. She proposes a NYC Climate Corps and recommends funding holistic green workforce pathways, prioritizing NYCHA residents, creating funded bridge programs into apprenticeships, using public sites as training sites, and tracking equity outcomes.
chapters.title: Cindy Manzanares (Green City Force) on NYC Climate Corps and NYCHA pathways

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: We train our graduates.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: Hello, my name is Cindy Manzanares.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: I'm the Chief Operating Officer of Green City Force.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: Green City Force runs an AmeriCorps program for young adults 16 to 24 who live in public housing and highly affordable housing.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: And we train them for careers in the green economy.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: Green City Forest has proposed a New York City Climate Corps as a practical vehicle for linking ambitious climate goals with workforce.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: Union construction careers are critical, but so are entry points in maintenance, operations, stormwater management, urban agriculture, zero waste composting, clean energy, building systems, energy efficiency, solar operations, and resilience.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: Many of these pathways can serve as bridges into more advanced training, apprenticeships, civil service, union pathways, and good jobs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: We'd like to offer the following recommendations.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: Fund holistic green workforce pathways, not only technical training.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: City investments should include outreach, recruitment, paid service, mental health support, case management, professional development, sector exposure,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: technical training, placement, and retention support.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: Prioritize NYCHA residents and frontline communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: Community hiring and place-based strategies should intentionally connect public climate investments to residents of communities

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: facing the greatest burdens from heat, flooding, pollution, and disinvestment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: Create funded bridge programs into apprenticeships, civil service, and advanced training.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: Young adults need supported on-ramps that help them move from readiness and exposure

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: into rigorous credentials, union pathways, and full-time employment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: Use public sites as workforce training sites.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: Schools, NYCHA campuses, city-owned buildings, public green infrastructure, and resilience projects should include funded roles for community-based workforce partners and track equity outcomes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: The city should measure NYCHA resident participation, credential attainment, work hours, placement retention.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Cindy Manzanares
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Jake Douglas Boren of the HOPE Program describes training New Yorkers overcoming homelessness, incarceration, and other barriers for green construction and building operations careers. He calls for requiring developers and contractors benefiting from city subsidies to partner with community-based training programs as a condition, and for investing in social enterprise models.
chapters.title: Jake Douglas Boren (HOPE Program) on green jobs for barriered New Yorkers

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: My name is Jaco Douglas-Born, Chief Program Officer at the HOPE Program, a 40-year-old workforce development organization in Hunts Point and downtown Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: We train New Yorkers overcoming the steepest barriers, homelessness, incarceration, domestic violence, long-term unemployment, especially from neighborhoods and communities facing compounding environmental harm.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: We prepare them for careers in green construction, building operations and maintenance, horticulture and cool roofs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: We are a founding member of the Green Economy Network.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: New York City has a jobs problem and a climate problem.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: The solution to one can help solve the other, but only if we are intentional about whose jobs we mean.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: Green jobs have something essential going for them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: They are here, they are accessible, and they pay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: They can be outsourced overseas, they don't require years of credentialing debt, and they are rooted in the same communities our participants come from.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: That's good climate policy and also a lifeline for New York City residents.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: The infrastructure for this work already exists in community-based organizations, workforce training providers, and labor partnerships that have spent decades proving it out.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: We should be front and center in delivering this plan, not competing at the margins of large public contracts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: Social enterprise is one powerful proof of that model.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: Enterprises generate revenue, hire program graduates directly, and meet real industry demands.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: All at once.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: It's a self-reinforcing cycle that doesn't depend on the next grant cycle.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: The city should be actively investing in and incentivizing this model.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: What we also ask, every developer, property owner, and contractor that benefits from city subsidy,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: tax incentives, capital funding, free services like cool roofs, should be required to partner with and hire from community-based training programs, not as a suggestion, as a condition.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: The coalitions are organized, the people we serve are ready, the city has begun to lay the groundwork, now it is time to go deeper.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Jake Douglas Boren
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Sofia Oliveira-Ritchie of Andromeda Community Initiative describes their free construction and green construction training for New Yorkers facing barriers, having trained 1,200+ individuals. She calls for stronger connections to employers and labor partners, expanded dedicated funding for green construction training, and continued publishing of green economy labor market data.
chapters.title: Sofia Oliveira-Ritchie (Andromeda Community Initiative) on green construction training

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sofia Oliveira-Ritchie
utterances.text: Good afternoon, chairs and members of the committee.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sofia Oliveira-Ritchie
utterances.text: My name is Sofia Oliveira-Ritchie, and I'm the Director of Development and Communications for the Andromeda Community Initiative, also known as ACI.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sofia Oliveira-Ritchie
utterances.text: We're a nonprofit workforce development organization that provides free construction and green construction training to New Yorkers facing barriers to employment.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sofia Oliveira-Ritchie
utterances.text: Since our founding, ACI has trained more than 1,200 individuals for careers in the construction industry.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sofia Oliveira-Ritchie
utterances.text: This includes folks returning home from incarceration, experiencing homelessness, navigating long-term unemployment and underemployment, and living in chronic poverty.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sofia Oliveira-Ritchie
utterances.text: Through industry-recognized certs, hands-on training, and comprehensive workforce development services, we help participants access family-sustaining careers and long-term economic mobility.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sofia Oliveira-Ritchie
utterances.text: I'm here today to highlight the critical role workforce development organizations play as New York City advances its energy efficiency, sustainability, and climate goals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sofia Oliveira-Ritchie
utterances.text: The demand for skilled workers who can support building retrofits, energy-efficient construction, electrification projects, and climate-focused infrastructure continues to grow.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sofia Oliveira-Ritchie
utterances.text: Through ACI's green training, developed with industry partners and aligned with emerging workforce needs, we are preparing New Yorkers for certs, technical knowledge, and workplace readiness needed to enter these growing sectors sustainably.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sofia Oliveira-Ritchie
utterances.text: However, training alone is not enough.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sofia Oliveira-Ritchie
utterances.text: Workforce providers need stronger connections to employers, developers, labor partners, and public sector projects to ensure that graduates can transition successfully.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sofia Oliveira-Ritchie
utterances.text: ACI currently has job-ready graduates who are eager to contribute to New York City's energy future, yet intentional hiring pathways are needed to connect trained workers to available opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sofia Oliveira-Ritchie
utterances.text: We encourage the city not only to continue investing in workforce development, but also to expand dedicated funding for green construction and workforce training programs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sofia Oliveira-Ritchie
utterances.text: We are already building the talent pipeline needed to support the objectives, but greater investment is needed to meet the scale of demand.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sofia Oliveira-Ritchie
utterances.text: We encourage the city to continue collecting, publishing, and sharing current labor information relevant to the green economy, including projections, anticipated hiring needs, wage data, and emerging industry trends.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Sofia Oliveira-Ritchie
utterances.text: Thank you again for the opportunity to testify.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Christopher Malone
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Next up, Travis Prolix.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Mikayla Crater and Stephen Levin from District 4.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: If anyone else is planning on testifying, please let the Sergeant at Arms know.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Travis Prew of Fordham University describes the Bronx Green Job Center as a collaborative model with educational partners, community agencies, and CBOs. He notes the Bronx lags in solar and EV infrastructure despite being the greenest borough, and urges a citywide investment strategy requiring collaboration and a centralized workforce authority.
chapters.title: Travis Prew (Fordham University) on Bronx Green Job Center

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Maloney.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: Chair Wan, good to see you.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: I'm Travis Prew.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: I'm Vice President for External Affairs at Fordham University.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: I have the privilege of creating and ultimately launching what's going to be the Bronx Green Job Center, which you heard a little bit about today.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: Really important conversation right now, especially in light of the federal and the state setbacks that you mentioned, Chair Wan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: Environmental justice is a moral imperative quarter.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: Our Jesuit values at Fordham University, and for us the mission is deeply local.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: Our Rose Hill campus is anchored in a community that's historically faced some of the highest poverty rates in the nation, as well as

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: born the brunt of disproportionate and severe environmental injustice.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: And while New York City's green economy is rapidly expanding, growth is bypassing the Bronx and many of the communities in outer boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: The disconnect is striking in the Bronx because we are the greenest borough, we are the youngest borough, and we have a legacy of community leaders who have driven citywide environmental action for decades.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: Yet the borough lags significantly in solar and electric vehicle infrastructure.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: Our community gardens remain...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: strained by a lack of scale and support.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: And the real crisis, however, is the lack of investment in people.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: Fordham bears a responsibility to convene stakeholders around a shared vision, any strategy for environmental justice, and with the partnership and guidance of EDC,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: UDC over the last year and a half, we've developed a collaborative public-private blueprint known as the Bronx Green Job Center, which is a partner-based model across three key areas.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: One is educational partners, so bringing in CUNY and SUNY to join with us in the delivery of curriculum.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: Two, communities and public agencies so that we ensure there's actually hiring pathways for trainees once they leave our work.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: And three, the community-based organizations that we know are trusted in the community that can provide that enrollment funnel and the wraparound services that folks need to succeed.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: So within my testimony, there are three specific areas that we have learned as part of this process that we urge action on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: One is implementing a citywide investment strategy that requires collaboration, including CBOs, as well as establishing a centralized workforce authority within the mayor's office.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Travis Prew
utterances.text: I'd be happy to answer any additional questions that you have.

chapters.summary: Michaela Crater of Southwest Brooklyn Industrial Development Corporation describes 40 years serving industrial companies in Sunset Park, Red Hook, and Gowanus, placing 2,000+ New Yorkers in jobs since 2017. She urges using existing industrial infrastructure for climate solutions, noting SBIDC has engaged 500+ businesses and referred 140 to offshore wind contractors, with 70% being MWBEs.
chapters.title: Michaela Crater (SBIDC) on industrial businesses in green economy

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michaela Crater
utterances.text: Good afternoon, my name is Michaela Crater and I'm speaking on behalf of Southwest Brooklyn Industrial Development Corporation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michaela Crater
utterances.text: Thank you chairs and the members of the committees for the opportunity to share our thoughts.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michaela Crater
utterances.text: SBIDC has served industrial companies and workers of Sunset Park, Red Hook, and Gowanus for over 40 years.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michaela Crater
utterances.text: Each year we provide direct technical assistance and advocacy to approximately 250 businesses and since 2017 we have helped place over 2,000 New Yorkers in equality family sustaining jobs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michaela Crater
utterances.text: Today we urge the committees to use their authority to ensure that the next chapter of the Green Economy Action Plan is implemented through the city's existing industrial infrastructure, the businesses that make, mend, move, and maintain New York.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michaela Crater
utterances.text: To ensure climate solutions are homegrown, the city must build on existing industrial assets.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michaela Crater
utterances.text: SBIDC and our partners across the five boroughs have a block-by-block knowledge of industrial business areas built over decades.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michaela Crater
utterances.text: We help businesses navigate city agencies and utilities, address quality of life concerns, connect companies to workforce and training resources, and serve as the trusted intermediary when agencies need our help.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michaela Crater
utterances.text: Across our green economy programs, SBIDC has actually engaged more than 500 businesses and referred 140 businesses to offshore wind contractors.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michaela Crater
utterances.text: 70% of those participating businesses are MWBEs.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michaela Crater
utterances.text: And we have demonstrated that targeted...

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michaela Crater
utterances.text: Industrial support can advance with climate-inclusive economic development goals.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michaela Crater
utterances.text: We urgently encourage the committees to continue and expand green economy investments, modernize industrial financing and business support tools, coordinate city policy around industrial implementation.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michaela Crater
utterances.text: New York City's industrial companies have operated through recessions, super storms, pandemics, and waves of displacement.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michaela Crater
utterances.text: The city should use this existing infrastructure to meet the climate challenge, create quality jobs, and ensure that the green economy is built by and for New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Michaela Crater
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Stephen Levin of Solar One describes their K-12 education, green workforce development, and renewable energy programs. He notes their LIC training center graduates 800 individuals annually with 3 certifications each. He recommends the city focus on engaging building management firms with green workforce providers and unions like 32BJ and Local 3 to ensure training meets employer needs.
chapters.title: Stephen Levin (Solar One) on green workforce development and building management engagement

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Levin
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chairs Maloney and Juan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Levin
utterances.text: My name is Stephen Levin.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Levin
utterances.text: I'm CEO of Solar One, a nonprofit organization which focuses on addressing the climate crisis through K-12 environmental education, green workforce development, renewable energy technical assistance for affordable housing, and the stewardship of Stuyvesant Cove Park in Councilmember Maloney's district,

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Levin
utterances.text: in which we just opened our new environmental education center.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Levin
utterances.text: Our office is in Councilmember Juan's district in Long Island City.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Levin
utterances.text: I've been honored to serve on the Green Economy Advisory Council for the last two years, which has helped to guide the beginning phase of the implementation of the Green Economy Action Plan.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Levin
utterances.text: Members of the Advisory Council came to the process with the goal of increasing our understanding of the challenges and opportunities that the clean energy transition provides.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Stephen Levin
utterances.text: And as you can imagine, having first met in late 2024, the landscape for clean energy has been ever-shifting nationally since then, and not in a good way.
